## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Define a convex function. State any two properties of convex functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**  
> A function $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined on a convex set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is called a **convex function** if for every $x_1, x_2 \in S$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:
>
> $$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

> **Two Properties (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**  
> **Property 1:** If $f$ is convex, then $-f$ is concave.  
> **Property 2:** If $f$ and $g$ are convex functions, then $f + g$ is also a convex function (sum of convex functions is convex).  
> *(Alternative accepted: Pointwise maximum of convex functions is convex; Epigraph of $f$ is a convex set.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**What is a non-convex function? Give one example and explain why it is non-convex.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**  
> A function is called **non-convex** if it does not satisfy the convexity condition for at least one pair of points. That is, there exist $x_1, x_2 \in S$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$ such that:
>
> $$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) > \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

> **Example with Justification (1.5 marks):**  
> Consider $f(x) = x^3$. We have $f''(x) = 6x$, which is negative for $x < 0$ and positive for $x > 0$. Since $f''(x)$ is not non-negative on the entire real line, $f(x) = x^3$ is **not convex globally**.  
> Concrete counterexample: $x_1 = -2$, $x_2 = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$ gives $f(-0.5) = -0.125 > -3.5 = 0.5 f(-2) + 0.5 f(1)$, violating the convexity inequality.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define a convex function. State and explain the first-order and second-order conditions for a differentiable function to be convex. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the second-order condition, determine whether the following functions are convex on the given domains: **(7 Marks)**  
    (i) $f(x) = e^{x^2}$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$  
    (ii) $f(x, y) = x^2 + 2xy + 2y^2 - 3x - 4y + 5$, $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Solution:**

**Definition of Convex Function (2 marks):**  
A function $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined on a convex set $S$ is **convex** if for all $x_1, x_2 \in S$ and all $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

Geometrically, the line segment (chord) joining any two points on the graph of $f$ lies on or above the graph.

**First-Order Condition (2.5 marks):**  
If $f$ is differentiable and convex on an open convex set $S$, then for all $x, y \in S$:

$$f(y) \ge f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y - x)$$

The expression $f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y - x)$ is the first-order Taylor approximation of $f$ at $x$. The condition states that the **entire graph lies above the tangent hyperplane** at every point. Conversely, if the above inequality holds for all $x, y \in S$, then $f$ is convex.

**Second-Order Condition (2.5 marks):**  
If $f \in C^2(S)$ (twice continuously differentiable), then $f$ is convex on $S$ if and only if its **Hessian matrix** is positive semi-definite everywhere in $S$:

$$\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0 \quad \forall x \in S$$

The Hessian $\nabla^2 f(x)$ is the matrix of second partial derivatives. Positive semi-definiteness means that for any vector $v$:

$$v^T \nabla^2 f(x) v \ge 0$$

This is equivalent to all eigenvalues of the Hessian being non-negative, or (in 2D) all principal minors being non-negative.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) $f(x) = e^{x^2}$ — Univariate Case (3.5 marks):**

Compute the first and second derivatives:

$$\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= e^{x^2} \cdot 2x = 2x e^{x^2} \\
f''(x) &= \frac{d}{dx}\left[2x e^{x^2}\right] = 2 e^{x^2} + 2x \cdot 2x e^{x^2} = 2e^{x^2}(1 + 2x^2)
\end{aligned}$$

Now analyse the sign of $f''(x)$:

- $e^{x^2} > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ (exponential is always positive)
- $(1 + 2x^2) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$

Therefore $f''(x) = 2e^{x^2}(1 + 2x^2) > 0$ for **all** $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

By the second-order condition, since $f''(x) \ge 0$ (in fact strictly positive), the function $f(x) = e^{x^2}$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$. $\blacksquare$

**(ii) $f(x, y) = x^2 + 2xy + 2y^2 - 3x - 4y + 5$ — Bivariate Case (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient (verify first derivatives exist):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_x &= 2x + 2y - 3 \\
f_y &= 2x + 4y - 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Hessian matrix:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{xx} &= 2, \quad f_{yy} = 4, \quad f_{xy} = f_{yx} = 2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\nabla^2 f = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Sylvester criterion (all leading principal minors non-negative):**

First principal minor:
$$M_1 = f_{xx} = 2 \ge 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Second principal minor (determinant of Hessian):
$$M_2 = \det(\nabla^2 f) = (2)(4) - (2)(2) = 8 - 4 = 4 \ge 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**  
Both principal minors are non-negative, so the Hessian is **positive semi-definite** (in fact positive definite since $M_2 > 0$). By the second-order condition, $f(x, y)$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}^2$. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** State and prove Jensen's inequality for a convex function. Hence deduce the AM-GM inequality for two positive numbers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of epigraph of a function. Show that $f(x) = \vert x \vert$ is convex by demonstrating that its epigraph is a convex set. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Solution:**

**Statement of Jensen's Inequality (2 marks):**  
If $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a convex function on a convex set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, and if $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k \in S$ are points with weights $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_k \ge 0$ satisfying $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i = 1$, then:

$$f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i x_i \right) \le \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i f(x_i)$$

**Proof by Mathematical Induction (4 marks):**

**Base Case ($k = 2$):**  
For $k = 2$, the inequality reduces to the very definition of convexity:

$$f(\lambda_1 x_1 + \lambda_2 x_2) \le \lambda_1 f(x_1) + \lambda_2 f(x_2)$$

with $\lambda_2 = 1 - \lambda_1$, $\lambda_1 \in [0,1]$. This holds by assumption that $f$ is convex. ✓

**Inductive Hypothesis:**  
Assume the inequality holds for some $k = m \ge 2$:

$$f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i x_i \right) \le \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i f(x_i), \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i = 1$$

**Inductive Step (Prove for $k = m + 1$):**  
We need to show:

$$f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i x_i \right) \le \sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i f(x_i)$$

Let $\Lambda = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i$. Since $\sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i = 1$ and $\lambda_{m+1} \ge 0$, we have $\Lambda \in (0, 1]$. Rewrite the convex combination as:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i x_i = \Lambda \cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda} x_i\right) + \lambda_{m+1} x_{m+1}$$

Let $\mu = \lambda_{m+1} \in [0, 1]$ and $(1 - \mu) = \Lambda$. Define the convex combination $\bar{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda} x_i$, which lies in $S$ (since $S$ is convex).

Apply the base case ($k = 2$) of convexity:

$$\begin{aligned}
f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i x_i\right) &= f(\mu \cdot x_{m+1} + (1-\mu) \cdot \bar{x}) \\
&\le \mu \cdot f(x_{m+1}) + (1-\mu) \cdot f(\bar{x}) \\
&= \lambda_{m+1} f(x_{m+1}) + \Lambda \cdot f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda} x_i\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Now apply the inductive hypothesis to the inner term:

$$f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda} x_i\right) \le \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda} f(x_i)$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i x_i\right) &\le \lambda_{m+1} f(x_{m+1}) + \Lambda \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\lambda_i}{\Lambda} f(x_i) \\
&= \lambda_{m+1} f(x_{m+1}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i f(x_i) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{m+1} \lambda_i f(x_i)
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the inductive step. Hence Jensen's inequality holds for all $k \ge 2$. $\blacksquare$

**Deduction of AM-GM Inequality (1 mark):**  
The exponential function $f(x) = e^{-x}$ is convex (since $f''(x) = e^{-x} > 0$). Applying Jensen's inequality with $n = 2$ and $x_1, x_2$ replaced by $-a, -b$ where $a, b > 0$, with $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0.5$:

$$e^{-(a+b)/2} = e^{0.5 \cdot (-a) + 0.5 \cdot (-b)} \le 0.5 e^{-a} + 0.5 e^{-b}$$

Multiplying by $e^{(a+b)/2}$:

$$1 \le 0.5 \left(e^{(b-a)/2} + e^{(a-b)/2}\right) = \cosh\left(\frac{a-b}{2}\right)$$

Through the more direct route: setting $f(x) = -\log x$ (which is convex on $\mathbb{R}_{++}$) and applying Jensen yields $\sqrt{ab} \le (a+b)/2$, the classical **AM-GM inequality**. ✓

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Definition of Epigraph (2 marks):**  
The **epigraph** of a function $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the set of all points $(x, t)$ in the extended space $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ such that the point lies on or above the graph of $f$:

$$\text{epi}(f) = \{(x, t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1} : x \in S, \; t \ge f(x)\}$$

Geometrically, the epigraph is the **region above the graph** of $f$, including the graph itself.

> **Fundamental Theorem:** A function $f$ is convex **if and only if** its epigraph is a convex set.

**Proof that $f(x) = \vert x \vert$ is Convex via Epigraph (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the epigraph of $f(x) = \vert x \vert$:**

$$\text{epi}(f) = \{(x, t) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : t \ge \vert x \vert \}$$

**Step 2 — Take two arbitrary points** $(x_1, t_1), (x_2, t_2) \in \text{epi}(f)$, so:

$$t_1 \ge \vert x_1 \vert \quad \text{and} \quad t_2 \ge \vert x_2 \vert$$

**Step 3 — Form a convex combination** with $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$(x^*, t^*) = \lambda(x_1, t_1) + (1-\lambda)(x_2, t_2) = (\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2,\; \lambda t_1 + (1-\lambda) t_2)$$

**Step 4 — Show $(x^*, t^*) \in \text{epi}(f)$, i.e., $t^* \ge \vert x^* \vert$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t^* &= \lambda t_1 + (1-\lambda) t_2 \\
&\ge \lambda \vert x_1 \vert + (1-\lambda) \vert x_2 \vert \quad \text{(since } t_1 \ge \vert x_1 \vert, \; t_2 \ge \vert x_2 \vert\text{)} \\
&\ge \vert \lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2 \vert \quad \text{(by the triangle inequality: } \vert \alpha a + \beta b \vert \le \vert \alpha \vert \vert a \vert + \vert \beta \vert \vert b \vert\text{)} \\
&= \vert x^* \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**  
Since the convex combination of any two points in $\text{epi}(f)$ also lies in $\text{epi}(f)$, the set $\text{epi}(f)$ is **convex**. By the fundamental theorem, $f(x) = \vert x \vert$ is a **convex function** on $\mathbb{R}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the domain check (–1 mark):** A function is only convex if its domain is a convex set. Always verify this *first* before applying the chord test.
> 2. **Reversing the inequality sign (–1 mark):** Convexity is $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$, **not** $\ge$. Mixing up convex and concave loses the chain of reasoning.
> 3. **Incomplete Hessian check (–2 marks):** For 2-variable functions, you must check **all three** conditions: $f_{xx} \ge 0$, $f_{yy} \ge 0$, and $f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 \ge 0$. Showing only the determinant is insufficient.
> 4. **Local vs. global claim (–1 mark):** If you find $f''(x) > 0$ for $x > 0$ but $f''(x) < 0$ for $x < 0$, the function is **not** convex globally — students often incorrectly call it convex.
> 5. **Missing counterexample for non-convexity (–1 mark):** To prove non-convexity, you must exhibit a *specific* pair of points and a specific $\lambda$ that violate the inequality. Stating "it does not satisfy the condition" without a counterexample is incomplete.
> 6. **AM-GM deduction error (–1 mark):** When deducing AM-GM from Jensen's, students often pick the wrong convex function. The correct choice is $f(x) = -\log x$ on $\mathbb{R}_{++}$, not $f(x) = x^2$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- [x] **Convex Function:** $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ for all $\lambda \in [0,1]$.
- [x] **Concave Function:** Reverse the inequality ($\ge$); equivalently, $-f$ is convex.
- [x] **Strictly Convex:** Inequality is strict for $x_1 \neq x_2$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$.
- [x] **Affine (Linear) Functions:** Both convex and concave (a "boundary" case).
- [x] **First-Order Condition:** $f(y) \ge f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y - x)$ for differentiable $f$.
- [x] **Second-Order Condition:** Hessian $\nabla^2 f(x)$ is positive semi-definite everywhere in the domain.
- [x] **Hessian in 2D:** $f_{xx} \ge 0$, $f_{yy} \ge 0$, $f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 \ge 0$ (all principal minors).
- [x] **Epigraph:** Region above the graph; convexity of $f$ $\iff$ convexity of $\text{epi}(f)$.
- [x] **Jensen's Inequality:** $f\left(\sum \lambda_i x_i\right) \le \sum \lambda_i f(x_i)$ with $\sum \lambda_i = 1$.
- [x] **Sum of Convex Functions:** Always convex; non-negative weighted sum is convex.
- [x] **Pointwise Maximum:** Convex if each $f_i$ is convex.
- [x] **Affine Composition:** $f(Ax + b)$ is convex if $f$ is convex.
- [x] **Convex Examples:** $x^2$, $e^x$, $\vert x \vert$, $-\log x$ (for $x > 0$), linear functions.
- [x] **Non-Convex Examples:** $x^3$ (globally), $\sin x$ (globally), $x^3 - 3x$ (multi-modal landscape).
- [x] **Optimisation Implication:** *Every local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum* — this is the single most important practical takeaway.
- [x] **Triangle Inequality Used:** $\vert \alpha a + \beta b \vert \le \vert \alpha \vert \vert a \vert + \vert \beta \vert \vert b \vert$ for $\alpha, \beta \ge 0$.

> [!TIP] **One-Line Exam Heuristic**
> If the Hessian is PSD everywhere → **convex**. If the Hessian has *any* negative eigenvalue at *any* point → **not convex**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving NLPs With One Variable (Unconstrained Maximization and Minimization)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solving NLPs With One Variable (Unconstrained Maximization and Minimization)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Unconstrained Non-Linear Programming (NLP) problem with one variable** is the problem of finding the value of a single decision variable $x$ that minimizes (or maximizes) a non-linear objective function $f(x)$ without any explicit constraints (other than the natural boundaries of the real line).

Formally stated:

$$\text{Find } x^* \text{ such that } f(x^*) \le f(x) \; \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \quad (\text{for minimization})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A point $x^*$ is a **global minimum** of $f(x)$ if $f(x^*) \le f(x)$ for all $x$ in the domain, and a **local minimum** if it holds only in a small neighbourhood $x^* - \delta \le x \le x^* + \delta$ for some $\delta > 0$.

### 1.2 The Concept of a Unimodal Function

A function $f(x)$ is said to be **unimodal** on the closed interval $[a, b]$ if there exists a unique optimal point $x^* \in [a, b]$ such that $f(x)$ is strictly monotonically decreasing on $[a, x^*]$ and strictly monotonically increasing on $[x^*, b]$ (for a minimum).

> [!NOTE]
> **Unimodality is the foundation** upon which all elimination-based search methods (Fibonacci, Golden Section, Interval Halving, Dichotomous) rely. If the function is multimodal, these methods may converge to a *local* optimum, not the *global* optimum.

### 1.3 Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Optimality

Let $f(x)$ be twice continuously differentiable ($C^2$) on the interval of interest.

| Condition | Statement | Interpretation |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **1st Order Necessary** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | Slope must vanish at any interior optimum (stationary point) |
| **2nd Order Sufficient (Min)** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$ | Concave-up (U-shaped) confirms local minimum |
| **2nd Order Sufficient (Max)** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$ | Concave-down (∩-shaped) confirms local maximum |
| **2nd Order Inconclusive** | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$ | Higher order derivatives must be examined (inflection point) |

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> **Analogy: Hiking in a Fogy Mountain**
> 
> Imagine you are blindfolded (you cannot see the whole terrain) and dropped somewhere on a single mountain ridge that has exactly **one peak** and **one valley** (a unimodal shape). You can only walk along one line — left or right. You want to find the *exact* lowest point of the valley.
> 
> - The **Golden Section method** is like always taking 61.8% of your remaining journey after every step.
> - The **Newton-Raphson method** is like a math-savvy hiker who uses the slope and curvature of the ground beneath his feet to leap directly towards the valley floor in a single, increasingly precise jump.
> - The **Bisection method** is like a cautious hiker who halves the search range after each probe, similar to finding a hidden number between 1 and 100 using the "higher/lower" game.

### 1.5 Real-World Engineering Examples

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Minimizing the weight of a tension spring subject to deflection (single design variable $d$ — wire diameter).
- **Electrical Engineering:** Maximizing power transfer from a source to a load by selecting a single resistance value.
- **Civil Engineering:** Optimizing the depth of a rectangular beam for minimum material cost.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Finding the optimal temperature that maximizes reaction yield in an isothermal batch reactor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Solution Methods

The solution methods for single-variable unconstrained NLPs are broadly classified into two major families:

```
SINGLE-VARIABLE UNCONSTRAINED OPTIMIZATION
├── A. ANALYTICAL (Calculus-Based) METHODS
│   ├── Solve f'(x) = 0 directly
│   └── Verify using f''(x) sign
│
└── B. NUMERICAL METHODS
    ├── 1. ELIMINATION (Direct Search) METHODS — Requires Unimodality
    │   ├── Unrestricted / Exhaustive Search
    │   ├── Bounding Phase Method
    │   ├── Interval Halving Method
    │   ├── Dichotomous Search Method
    │   ├── Fibonacci Search Method
    │   └── Golden Section Search Method
    │
    └── 2. INTERPOLATION (Indirect) METHODS — Fits polynomial to function
        ├── Quadratic Interpolation (Powell's Method)
        ├── Cubic Interpolation
        ├── Newton-Raphson Method
        ├── Bisection Method (applied to f'(x))
        └── Secant Method (applied to f'(x))
```

### 2.2 Why and How Each Method Works

- **Elimination Methods** — Repeatedly *shrink* the interval $[a, b]$ containing the optimum by comparing function values at interior points. Convergence is guaranteed but linear. They do **not** require derivatives.
- **Interpolation Methods** — Fit a *low-order polynomial* (quadratic, cubic) through sample points and analytically find the polynomial's optimum. They use *curvature* information and converge **much faster** (quadratic or superlinear) near the solution, but may diverge if started far from the optimum.
- **Newton-Raphson** — Solves $f'(x) = 0$ using the *first two derivatives*. Quadratic convergence if the starting point is good and $f''(x^*) \neq 0$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Method** | **Core Formula** | **Convergence** | **Function Evals per Iter** | **Key Parameter** |
|------------|------------------|-----------------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| **Golden Section** | $x_1 = a + 0.382(b-a), \quad x_2 = a + 0.618(b-a)$ | Linear | 1 (after first iter) | $\rho = \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} \approx \mathbf{0.618}$ |
| **Fibonacci** | $L_k = \frac{F_{n-k+1}}{F_{n+1}} L_0$ | Linear | 1 (after first iter) | $F_{n+1} > \frac{L_0}{\varepsilon}$ |
| **Interval Halving** | $x_m = \tfrac{a+b}{2}, \quad x_1 = x_m - \tfrac{L}{4}, \quad x_2 = x_m + \tfrac{L}{4}$ | Linear | 2 | $L_{k+1} = \tfrac{L_k}{2}$ |
| **Dichotomous** | $x_1 = \tfrac{a+b}{2} - \tfrac{\delta}{2}, \quad x_2 = \tfrac{a+b}{2} + \tfrac{\delta}{2}$ | Linear | 2 | Tiny $\delta > 0$ |
| **Newton-Raphson** | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ | Quadratic | 0 (uses derivatives) | Initial guess $x_0$ |
| **Bisection (on $f'$)** | $x_m = \tfrac{a+b}{2}$ | Linear | 0 (uses $f'$ values) | $L_{k+1} = \tfrac{L_k}{2}$ |
| **Secant** | $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k) \cdot \dfrac{x_{k-1} - x_k}{f'(x_{k-1}) - f'(x_k)}$ | Superlinear ($\approx 1.618$) | 0 (uses $f'$ values) | Two initial guesses |
| **Quadratic Interp.** | $x_4 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_1(x_2^2 - x_3^2) + f_2(x_3^2 - x_1^2) + f_3(x_1^2 - x_2^2)}{f_1(x_2 - x_3) + f_2(x_3 - x_1) + f_3(x_1 - x_2)}$ | Superlinear | 3 (initial) + 1 per iter | Three points needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Tolerance Notation:** $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) denotes the **pre-specified stopping tolerance** on the interval length. The algorithm halts when $L_k = b_k - a_k \le \varepsilon$ (or pre-defined number of iterations for Fibonacci). The **initial uncertainty reduction ratio** is often written as $\tfrac{L_n}{L_0}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Golden Section** is the workhorse of industrial **process control tuning** (Ziegler-Nicholas-like heuristics).
- **Newton-Raphson** is the *de facto* algorithm inside every **root-finder of CAD/CAE software** (ANSYS, COMSOL) and **machine learning optimizers** (deep learning loss minimization).
- **Quadratic Interpolation** is heavily used in **line search sub-problems** of multi-dimensional algorithms like BFGS and Newton's method in many variables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical (Calculus) Solution — Complete Walkthrough

**Problem:** Find the maximum and minimum of $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$.

**Step 1: Compute the first derivative.**

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$$

**Step 2: Apply the First-Order Necessary Condition $f'(x^*) = 0$.**

$$6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 0$$

Dividing by 6:

$$x^2 - 3x + 2 = 0$$

Factoring the quadratic:

$$(x-1)(x-2) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x_1 = 1, \; x_2 = 2$$

**Step 3: Compute the second derivative.**

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(6x^2 - 18x + 12) = 12x - 18$$

**Step 4: Apply the Second-Order Sufficiency Test at each stationary point.**

- At $x_1 = 1$: $\quad f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = -6 < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{local MAXIMUM}$
- At $x_2 = 2$: $\quad f''(2) = 12(2) - 18 = 6 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{local MINIMUM}$

**Step 5: Compute the function values at the optimal points.**

$$f(1) = 2(1) - 9(1) + 12(1) + 3 = 2 - 9 + 12 + 3 = \mathbf{8 \; (\text{MAX value})}$$

$$f(2) = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) + 3 = 16 - 36 + 24 + 3 = \mathbf{7 \; (\text{MIN value})}$$

**Final Result:** Local maximum $= 8$ at $x = 1$; Local minimum $= 7$ at $x = 2$.

---

### 3.2 Newton-Raphson Method — Exhaustive Derivation

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ starting from $x_0 = 2.5$, with stopping criterion $\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert \le 10^{-3}$.

**Algorithm Foundation:** The minimum of $f(x)$ occurs where $f'(x) = 0$. Apply Newton-Raphson to the function $g(x) = f'(x)$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{g(x_k)}{g'(x_k)} = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

**Prerequisites (computed above):** $f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$ and $f''(x) = 12x - 18$.

**Iteration 1 (k = 0, $x_0 = 2.5$):**

$$f'(2.5) = 6(6.25) - 18(2.5) + 12 = 37.5 - 45 + 12 = 4.5$$

$$f''(2.5) = 12(2.5) - 18 = 30 - 18 = 12$$

$$x_1 = 2.5 - \frac{4.5}{12} = 2.5 - 0.375 = \mathbf{2.125}$$

Error: $\vert 2.5 - 2.125 \vert = 0.375 > 10^{-3}$. **Continue.**

**Iteration 2 (k = 1, $x_1 = 2.125$):**

$$f'(2.125) = 6(2.125)^2 - 18(2.125) + 12 = 6(4.5156) - 38.25 + 12 = 27.0938 - 38.25 + 12 = 0.8438$$

$$f''(2.125) = 12(2.125) - 18 = 25.5 - 18 = 7.5$$

$$x_2 = 2.125 - \frac{0.8438}{7.5} = 2.125 - 0.1125 = \mathbf{2.0125}$$

Error: $\vert 2.125 - 2.0125 \vert = 0.1125 > 10^{-3}$. **Continue.**

**Iteration 3 (k = 2, $x_2 = 2.0125$):**

$$f'(2.0125) = 6(2.0125)^2 - 18(2.0125) + 12 = 6(4.0502) - 36.225 + 12 = 24.3012 - 36.225 + 12 = 0.0762$$

$$f''(2.0125) = 12(2.0125) - 18 = 24.15 - 18 = 6.15$$

$$x_3 = 2.0125 - \frac{0.0762}{6.15} = 2.0125 - 0.0124 = \mathbf{2.0001}$$

Error: $\vert 2.0125 - 2.0001 \vert = 0.0124 > 10^{-3}$. **Continue.**

**Iteration 4 (k = 3, $x_3 = 2.0001$):**

$$f'(2.0001) = 6(2.0001)^2 - 18(2.0001) + 12 = 6(4.0004) - 36.0018 + 12 = 24.0024 - 36.0018 + 12 = 0.0006$$

$$f''(2.0001) = 12(2.0001) - 18 = 24.0012 - 18 = 6.0012$$

$$x_4 = 2.0001 - \frac{0.0006}{6.0012} \approx \mathbf{2.00000}$$

Error: $\vert 2.0001 - 2.0000 \vert \approx 0.0001 \le 10^{-3}$. **STOP.**

**Converged Result:** $x^* \approx 2.0000$ with $f(x^*) = 7$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Speed:** Notice the error pattern: $0.375 \to 0.1125 \to 0.0124 \to 0.0001$. The number of correct digits approximately *doubles* each iteration. This is the hallmark of **quadratic convergence**.

---

### 3.3 Golden Section Search — Exhaustive Derivation

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x-2)^2 + 0.5$ on $[a_0, b_0] = [0, 5]$ with $\varepsilon = 0.05$.

**Setup:** The Golden Ratio constant is $\rho = \frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} = \mathbf{0.618034}$.

**Iteration 1:** $L_0 = b_0 - a_0 = 5 - 0 = 5$.

$$x_1 = a_0 + (1-\rho)(b_0 - a_0) = 0 + 0.382(5) = 1.910$$

$$x_2 = a_0 + \rho(b_0 - a_0) = 0 + 0.618(5) = 3.090$$

$$f(1.910) = (1.910 - 2)^2 + 0.5 = 0.0081 + 0.5 = 0.5081$$

$$f(3.090) = (3.090 - 2)^2 + 0.5 = 1.1881 + 0.5 = 1.6881$$

Since $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, the minimum lies in $[a_0, x_2] = [0, 3.090]$. The new $x_1$ is the old $x_2$ (i.e., $x_2$ becomes the right interior point of the new interval). Set $b_1 = 3.090$.

**Iteration 2:** $L_1 = 3.090 - 0 = 3.090$.

$$x_1^{(\text{new})} = a_1 + (1-\rho)(b_1 - a_1) = 0 + 0.382(3.090) = 1.180$$

$$x_2 = 1.910 \text{ (reused from previous iteration)}$$

$$f(1.180) = (1.180 - 2)^2 + 0.5 = 0.6724 + 0.5 = 1.1724$$

$$f(1.910) = 0.5081$$

Since $f(x_2) < f(x_1^{(\text{new})})$, the minimum lies in $[x_1^{(\text{new})}, b_1] = [1.180, 3.090]$. Set $a_2 = 1.180$.

**Iteration 3:** $L_2 = 3.090 - 1.180 = 1.910$.

$$x_1 = 1.180 + 0.382(1.910) = 1.910 \text{ (reused)}$$

$$x_2^{(\text{new})} = 1.180 + 0.618(1.910) = 2.360$$

$$f(1.910) = 0.5081$$

$$f(2.360) = (2.360 - 2)^2 + 0.5 = 0.1296 + 0.5 = 0.6296$$

Since $f(x_1) < f(x_2^{(\text{new})})$, the minimum lies in $[1.180, 2.360]$. Set $b_3 = 2.360$.

**Iteration 4:** $L_3 = 2.360 - 1.180 = 1.180$.

Continuing this process, after many iterations $L_k \to 0$ and the interval brackets the true optimum $x^* = 2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Golden" Property:** Notice that the new interior point is always the OLD interior point. This is why Golden Section needs only **ONE new function evaluation per iteration** after the first iteration — the largest efficiency among elimination methods that need 2 points per interval.

---

### 3.4 Quadratic Interpolation — Exhaustive Derivation

**Problem:** Find the minimum of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$ using initial sample points $x_0 = 0, x_1 = 1, x_2 = 2$.

**Step 1: Evaluate the function at the three sample points.**

$$f(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 + 1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = 1$$

$$f(1) = 1 - 6 + 9 + 1 = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_1 = 5$$

$$f(2) = 8 - 24 + 18 + 1 = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_2 = 3$$

**Step 2: Apply the quadratic interpolation formula to find the polynomial's minimum $x_3$.**

$$x_3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{f_0(x_1^2 - x_2^2) + f_1(x_2^2 - x_0^2) + f_2(x_0^2 - x_1^2)}{f_0(x_1 - x_2) + f_1(x_2 - x_0) + f_2(x_0 - x_1)}$$

Numerator:

$$N = 1(1^2 - 2^2) + 5(2^2 - 0^2) + 3(0^2 - 1^2)$$

$$N = 1(1 - 4) + 5(4 - 0) + 3(0 - 1) = 1(-3) + 5(4) + 3(-1) = -3 + 20 - 3 = 14$$

Denominator:

$$D = 1(1 - 2) + 5(2 - 0) + 3(0 - 1) = 1(-1) + 5(2) + 3(-1) = -1 + 10 - 3 = 6$$

Therefore:

$$x_3 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{14}{6} = \frac{14}{12} = \mathbf{1.1667}$$

**Step 3: Verify by computing $f(x_3)$.**

$$f(1.1667) = (1.1667)^3 - 6(1.1667)^2 + 9(1.1667) + 1 = 1.588 - 8.167 + 10.500 + 1 = 4.921$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CAUTION:** The true minimum of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1$ is at $x^* = 3$ (with $f(3) = 1$). Our interpolated result $x_3 = 1.167$ corresponds to a *local maximum* ($f''(1) = -6 < 0$). The interpolation converged to the wrong extremum because the initial points bracketed the wrong valley. **In KTU exams, always verify the result using $f''(x_3) > 0$ and consider a second iteration with a wider sampling range.**

---

### 3.5 Bisection Method Applied to $f'(x) = 0$

**Problem:** Find the minimum of $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ in the interval $[0, 3]$ using the bisection method on $f'(x)$.

**Step 1:** $f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$. Evaluate at endpoints:

$$f'(0) = 12 > 0, \quad f'(3) = 6(9) - 54 + 12 = 54 - 54 + 12 = 12 > 0$$

Both are positive — no sign change! **The minimum is NOT bracketed.** Choose a better interval, say $[1.5, 2.5]$:

$$f'(1.5) = 6(2.25) - 18(1.5) + 12 = 13.5 - 27 + 12 = -1.5 < 0$$

$$f'(2.5) = 4.5 > 0$$

Sign change exists. Root lies in $[1.5, 2.5]$.

**Iteration 1:** $x_m = \frac{1.5 + 2.5}{2} = 2.0$. $\quad f'(2.0) = 0$.

Since $f'(2.0) = 0$ exactly, the minimum is at $x^* = 2.0$ (which matches the analytical answer!).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bisection is reliable but slow:** It needs $n = \log_2\left(\frac{L_0}{\varepsilon}\right)$ iterations, where $L_0$ is the initial interval length. Unlike Newton-Raphson, it never overshoots or diverges.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Single-Variable NLP Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single-Variable Unconstrained NLP] --> B{Analytical Method?}
    A --> C{Numerical Method?}
    B --> B1[Solve f prime of x equals 0]
    B1 --> B2[Verify with f double prime]
    C --> D{Requires Derivatives?}
    D --> E1[Elimination Methods]
    D --> E2[Interpolation Methods]
    E1 --> F1[Unrestricted Search]
    E1 --> F2[Bounding Phase Method]
    E1 --> F3[Interval Halving]
    E1 --> F4[Dichotomous Search]
    E1 --> F5[Fibonacci Search]
    E1 --> F6[Golden Section Search]
    E2 --> G1[Quadratic Interpolation]
    E2 --> G2[Cubic Interpolation]
    E2 --> G3[Newton-Raphson]
    E2 --> G4[Secant Method]
    E2 --> G5[Bisection on f prime]
    F5 --> H1[Uses Fibonacci Numbers]
    F5 --> H2[Reduction ratio varies]
    F6 --> I1[Constant ratio 0.618]
    F6 --> I2[One new eval per iter]
    G3 --> J1[Quadratic Convergence]
    G3 --> J2[Needs f prime and f double prime]
    G5 --> K1[Linear Convergence]
    G5 --> K2[Bracketing Method]
```

### 4.2 Golden Section Search — Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Define f of x, interval a0 b0, tolerance eps] --> S2[Compute rho equals 0.618]
    S2 --> S3[Set x1 equals a plus 0.382 times L0]
    S3 --> S4[Set x2 equals a plus 0.618 times L0]
    S4 --> S5[Compute f of x1 and f of x2]
    S5 --> S6{Is f of x1 less than f of x2?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[New interval a1 equals a, b1 equals x2]
    S6 -- No --> S8[New interval a1 equals x1, b1 equals b]
    S7 --> S9[Recompute x1 and x2 using new interval]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10{Is b minus a less than eps?}
    S10 -- No --> S6
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Optimal x equals midpoint of a and b]
    S11 --> S12[End]
```

### 4.3 Newton-Raphson Method — Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Start: Define f of x, initial guess x0, tolerance eps] --> N2[Compute f prime of x0 and f double prime of x0]
    N2 --> N3{Is f double prime of x0 equal to 0?}
    N3 -- Yes --> N4[STOP: Method Fails]
    N3 -- No --> N5[Compute x1 equals x0 minus f prime of x0 over f double prime of x0]
    N5 --> N6{Is absolute difference less than eps?}
    N6 -- No --> N7[Set x0 equals x1]
    N7 --> N2
    N6 -- Yes --> N8[Optimal x equals x1]
    N8 --> N9[End]
```

### 4.4 Convergence Rate Comparison (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Convergence Speed Hierarchy] --> B1[SLOWEST: Bisection Linear]
    A --> B2[INTERVAL HALVING Linear]
    A --> B3[GOLDEN SECTION Linear]
    A --> B4[FIBONACCI Linear]
    A --> B5[SECANT Superlinear 1.618]
    A --> B6[QUADRATIC INTERPOLATION Superlinear]
    A --> B7[FASTEST: NEWTON RAPHSON Quadratic]
    B1 --> C1[Halves interval each step]
    B2 --> C2[Halves interval each step]
    B3 --> C3[Reduces by 0.618 each step]
    B4 --> C4[Uses Fibonacci ratios]
    B5 --> C5[Approx 1.618 order]
    B6 --> C6[Fits quadratic polynomial]
    B7 --> C7[Doubles correct digits each step]
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree — Which Method to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the function unimodal?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is analytical solution feasible?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Sample widely, use global search like Genetic Algorithm]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Use Newton-Raphson for fastest convergence]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5{Are derivatives available?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Newton-Raphson or Secant]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[Use Golden Section or Fibonacci]
    Q7 --> Q8{Is the number of iterations fixed?}
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q9[Use Fibonacci Search]
    Q8 -->|No| Q10[Use Golden Section]
    Q9 --> R1[End Recommendation]
    Q10 --> R1
    Q6 --> R1
    Q4 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> *State and explain the necessary and sufficient conditions for a point $x^*$ to be a local minimum of a twice-differentiable function $f(x)$. [3 Marks]*

**Model Answer:**

The **first-order necessary condition** states that if $x^*$ is a local minimum of $f(x)$ in the interior of the domain, then the first derivative must vanish: $f'(x^*) = 0$ (point must be stationary).

The **second-order sufficient condition** requires two conditions simultaneously:
1. $f'(x^*) = 0$ (first derivative is zero at the candidate point).
2. $f''(x^*) > 0$ (second derivative is strictly positive, confirming the function is locally convex / U-shaped at $x^*$).

If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$, then $x^*$ is a *local maximum*. If $f''(x^*) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** and higher-order derivatives must be examined. **(3 marks — 1 for necessary, 2 for sufficient conditions with sign interpretation)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define a "unimodal function" on a closed interval $[a, b]$ and explain its importance in the context of elimination-based search methods. [3 Marks]*

**Model Answer:**

A function $f(x)$ is said to be **unimodal** on the closed interval $[a, b]$ if there exists a unique point $x^* \in [a, b]$ such that $f(x)$ is strictly monotonically decreasing on $[a, x^*]$ and strictly monotonically increasing on $[x^*, b]$ (assuming a minimization problem).

**Importance:** Unimodality guarantees that any elimination method (Golden Section, Fibonacci, Interval Halving, Dichotomous) which progressively reduces the bracketing interval $[a, b]$ will **always** retain the global optimum inside the new, smaller interval. Without unimodality, these methods may converge to a *local* optimum that is not the *global* optimum, since the reduced interval could exclude the true global solution. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for importance to elimination methods)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
> 
> **(a)** Find the maximum and minimum values of $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ using the necessary and sufficient conditions. Comment on the nature of the extremum at each stationary point. **[7 Marks]**
> 
> **(b)** Minimize $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ using the Newton-Raphson method starting from $x_0 = 2.5$. Perform iterations until $\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert \le 0.01$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Step 1:** Compute $f'(x)$ and set to zero.

$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x^2 - 3x + 2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (x-1)(x-2) = 0$$

So stationary points are $x_1 = 1$ and $x_2 = 2$.

**Step 2:** Compute $f''(x)$ and evaluate at stationary points.

$$f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

$$f''(1) = 12 - 18 = -6 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1 \text{ is a LOCAL MAXIMUM}$$

$$f''(2) = 24 - 18 = 6 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 \text{ is a LOCAL MINIMUM}$$

**Step 3:** Compute function values.

$$f(1) = 2(1) - 9(1) + 12(1) + 3 = 2 - 9 + 12 + 3 = \mathbf{8 \;\; (\text{MAX})}$$

$$f(2) = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) + 3 = 16 - 36 + 24 + 3 = \mathbf{7 \;\; (\text{MIN})}$$

**Final Answer:** Local Maximum $= 8$ at $x = 1$; Local Minimum $= 7$ at $x = 2$. **(7 marks — 2 for derivative, 2 for second derivative test, 2 for function values, 1 for conclusion)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Formula:** $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$, where $f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$ and $f''(x) = 12x - 18$.

**Iteration 1** ($x_0 = 2.5$):

$$f'(2.5) = 6(6.25) - 18(2.5) + 12 = 37.5 - 45 + 12 = 4.5$$

$$f''(2.5) = 12(2.5) - 18 = 12$$

$$x_1 = 2.5 - \frac{4.5}{12} = 2.5 - 0.375 = 2.125$$

Error: $\vert 2.5 - 2.125 \vert = 0.375 > 0.01$ — **Continue**.

**Iteration 2** ($x_1 = 2.125$):

$$f'(2.125) = 6(4.5156) - 18(2.125) + 12 = 27.0938 - 38.25 + 12 = 0.8438$$

$$f''(2.125) = 25.5 - 18 = 7.5$$

$$x_2 = 2.125 - \frac{0.8438}{7.5} = 2.125 - 0.1125 = 2.0125$$

Error: $\vert 2.125 - 2.0125 \vert = 0.1125 > 0.01$ — **Continue**.

**Iteration 3** ($x_2 = 2.0125$):

$$f'(2.0125) = 6(4.0502) - 36.225 + 12 = 24.3012 - 36.225 + 12 = 0.0762$$

$$f''(2.0125) = 24.15 - 18 = 6.15$$

$$x_3 = 2.0125 - \frac{0.0762}{6.15} = 2.0125 - 0.0124 = 2.0001$$

Error: $\vert 2.0125 - 2.0001 \vert = 0.0124 > 0.01$ — **Continue**.

**Iteration 4** ($x_3 = 2.0001$):

$$f'(2.0001) \approx 0.0006, \quad f''(2.0001) \approx 6.0012$$

$$x_4 = 2.0001 - \frac{0.0006}{6.0012} \approx 2.0000$$

Error: $\vert 2.0001 - 2.0000 \vert \approx 0.0001 \le 0.01$ — **STOP**.

**Converged Answer:** $x^* \approx 2.00$, with $f(x^*) = 7$. **(7 marks — 1 for formula, 2 per iteration × 2 iterations shown in detail, 1 for stopping criterion, 1 for final answer)**

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question]**
> 
> **(a)** Minimize $f(x) = (x-2)^2 + 0.5$ on the interval $[0, 5]$ using the **Golden Section Search method** until the interval length $L_k \le 0.1$. Show all iterations in a tabular form. **[7 Marks]**
> 
> **(b)** Describe the **Newton-Raphson method** for finding the minimum of $f(x)$. State the formula, the convergence property, and one major pitfall of this method. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

Use $\rho = 0.618$, so $1 - \rho = 0.382$.

| **Iter $k$** | $a_k$ | $b_k$ | $L_k$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $f(x_1)$ | $f(x_2)$ | **Decision** |
|--------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| 0 | 0.000 | 5.000 | 5.000 | 1.910 | 3.090 | 0.5081 | 1.6881 | $f(x_1) < f(x_2) \Rightarrow b = x_2$ |
| 1 | 0.000 | 3.090 | 3.090 | 1.180 | 1.910 | 1.1724 | 0.5081 | $f(x_2) < f(x_1) \Rightarrow a = x_1$ |
| 2 | 1.180 | 3.090 | 1.910 | 1.910 | 2.360 | 0.5081 | 0.6296 | $f(x_1) < f(x_2) \Rightarrow b = x_2$ |
| 3 | 1.180 | 2.360 | 1.180 | 1.630 | 1.910 | 0.5369 | 0.5081 | $f(x_2) < f(x_1) \Rightarrow a = x_1$ |
| 4 | 1.630 | 2.360 | 0.730 | 1.910 | 2.080 | 0.5081 | 0.5064 | $f(x_2) < f(x_1) \Rightarrow a = x_1$ |
| 5 | 1.910 | 2.360 | 0.450 | 2.080 | 2.190 | 0.5064 | 0.5361 | $f(x_1) < f(x_2) \Rightarrow b = x_2$ |
| 6 | 1.910 | 2.190 | 0.280 | 2.020 | 2.080 | 0.5004 | 0.5064 | $f(x_1) < f(x_2) \Rightarrow b = x_2$ |
| 7 | 1.910 | 2.080 | 0.170 | 1.970 | 2.020 | 0.5009 | 0.5004 | $f(x_2) < f(x_1) \Rightarrow a = x_1$ |
| 8 | 1.970 | 2.080 | 0.110 | 2.020 | 2.030 | 0.5004 | 0.5009 | $f(x_1) < f(x_2) \Rightarrow b = x_2$ |
| 9 | 1.970 | 2.030 | **0.060** | — | — | — | — | **STOP** ($L \le 0.1$) |

**Final Answer:** $x^* \approx \frac{1.970 + 2.030}{2} = 2.00$, with $f(x^*) = 0.5$. **(7 marks — 1 for setup, 4 for table, 1 for decision logic, 1 for final answer)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Algorithm:** To find the minimum of $f(x)$, we solve the equation $f'(x) = 0$ using the Newton-Raphson iteration:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

**Derivation Concept:** A Taylor series expansion of $f'(x)$ around $x_k$ gives $f'(x_{k+1}) \approx f'(x_k) + f''(x_k)(x_{k+1} - x_k) = 0$. Solving for $x_{k+1}$ yields the NR formula.

**Convergence Property:** **Quadratic convergence** — the number of correct decimal digits approximately *doubles* with every iteration, provided:
- $f''(x^*) \neq 0$ at the true minimum.
- The initial guess $x_0$ is sufficiently close to $x^*$.

**Major Pitfall:** Newton-Raphson can **diverge** or converge to a **local maximum / saddle point** if:
- $f''(x_k) = 0$ at some iteration (division by zero).
- The starting point is far from the true minimum and the function has multiple extrema.
- The function is not sufficiently smooth.

**Remedies:** Use a safeguarding strategy (e.g., switch to bisection if NR overshoots), or use damped Newton: $x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k \cdot \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ with step size $\alpha_k \in (0, 1]$. **(7 marks — 2 for formula + concept, 2 for convergence property, 3 for pitfall discussion)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Second-Derivative Test:** Many students compute $f'(x) = 0$ and stop. The KTU board **always** awards 2-3 marks for verifying whether the stationary point is a minimum, maximum, or saddle point using $f''(x)$. **Always include this step.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Newton-Raphson with the Bisection Method Formula:** Newton-Raphson uses $f'(x_k) / f''(x_k)$ with **two derivatives**. Bisection uses the **sign change** of $f'(x)$ across the midpoint. These are *not* interchangeable.
> 
> 3. **Not Reusing the Interior Point in Golden Section:** In the Golden Section table, the *old* $x_1$ or $x_2$ becomes the *new* interior point. Forgetting this and re-evaluating the function wastes iterations and costs marks.
> 
> 4. **Quadratic Interpolation Result Mismatch:** If the interpolated $x_4$ falls outside the bracketing interval $[x_1, x_3]$, it indicates the initial points are too far from the true minimum. KTU expects the student to *re-choose points* and *repeat the procedure*.
> 
> 5. **No Units in Engineering Problems:** When solving applied problems, **always state units** (e.g., diameter in mm, cost in ₹). The KTU board explicitly allocates 1 mark for unit consistency.
> 
> 6. **Tolerance Criterion Mismatch:** Different problems use different stopping criteria: $\vert x_{k+1} - x_k \vert \le \varepsilon$ OR $L_k = b_k - a_k \le \varepsilon$. **Read the problem statement carefully.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **single-variable unconstrained NLP** seeks the value of $x$ that minimizes or maximizes $f(x)$ without constraints.
- **Necessary condition** for interior optimum: $f'(x^*) = 0$.
- **Sufficient condition for minimum**: $f'(x^*) = 0$ AND $f''(x^*) > 0$.
- **Sufficient condition for maximum**: $f'(x^*) = 0$ AND $f''(x^*) < 0$.
- **Unimodal functions** have exactly one optimum in the search interval and are required for elimination methods.
- **Elimination methods** (Golden Section, Fibonacci) progressively shrink the interval and need no derivatives.
- **Golden Section** uses the constant ratio $\rho \approx \mathbf{0.618}$ and needs only **1 new function evaluation per iteration**.
- **Fibonacci** uses the ratio $F_{n-k+1}/F_{n+1}$ and the number of iterations $n$ must be pre-decided.
- **Newton-Raphson formula**: $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$, exhibits **quadratic convergence**, but may diverge.
- **Secant method** is a derivative-free variant of Newton-Raphson with superlinear convergence.
- **Bisection method** on $f'(x)$ halves the interval each step — slow but guaranteed.
- **Quadratic interpolation** uses 3 points to fit a parabola and find its minimum analytically using the standard formula involving $f_0, f_1, f_2$ and $x_0, x_1, x_2$.
- Always **verify** the final answer using the second-derivative test, $f''(x^*) > 0$ for minimization.
- Always state the **stopping criterion used** and confirm it is satisfied in the final iteration.
- For KTU 2024 ESE: structure 14-mark answers as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** with full tabular work and explicit verification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unconstrained Maximization and Minimization With Several Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unconstrained Maximization and Minimization With Several Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unconstrained Optimisation (Multivariable):** Given a real-valued function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, unconstrained optimisation seeks a point $\mathbf{x}^* = (x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*)$ such that $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \le f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (**Minimisation**) or $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \ge f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (**Maximisation**), with no restrictions imposed on $\mathbf{x}$.

For several variables ($n \ge 2$), the analysis extends from single-variable calculus to **vector calculus** and **matrix algebra**, requiring the use of the **gradient vector** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ and the **Hessian matrix** $H(\mathbf{x})$.

## 1.2 Types of Optima

- **Global Optimum**: The best value of $f$ over the *entire* domain $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Local Optimum**: The best value of $f$ within a *neighbourhood* $N_\epsilon(\mathbf{x}^*)$ of the point.
  - **Local Minimum** $\mathbf{x}^*$: $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \le f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ near $\mathbf{x}^*$.
  - **Local Maximum** $\mathbf{x}^*$: $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \ge f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ near $\mathbf{x}^*$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Hiking on a Mountain Range"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are standing somewhere on a vast mountain range with multiple peaks and valleys (the function surface). Unconstrained optimisation is the task of finding either the **highest peak** (maximisation) or the **deepest valley** (minimisation) using only the local information of the slope and curvature beneath your feet.
> - The **gradient** $\nabla f$ is the direction of the steepest ascent — your compass pointing uphill.
> - The **Hessian** $H$ tells you whether the terrain under you is bowl-shaped (valley), dome-shaped (peak), or saddle-shaped.
> - **Stationary points** are flat spots where the gradient vanishes — tops, bottoms, or saddles.

## 1.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Machine Learning:** Training a neural network is unconstrained minimisation of a loss function in millions of variables.
- **Structural Design:** Minimising weight subject to no explicit restrictions (constraints added later).
- **Economics:** Utility maximisation and cost minimisation.
- **Signal Processing:** Wiener filter design.

## 1.5 Standard Notation Table

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Design / decision variable vector |
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Scalar objective function |
| $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ | Gradient vector $(n \times 1)$ |
| $H(\mathbf{x})$ | Hessian matrix $(n \times n)$ |
| $\mathbf{x}^*$ | Optimal / stationary point |
| $f^{**}$ or $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ | Optimal function value |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Necessary Conditions for a Local Optimum (First-Order)

A necessary condition for $\mathbf{x}^*$ to be a local optimum of a differentiable $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

This is called a **stationary point** (or **critical point**). The gradient vector is:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[2pt] \vdots \\[2pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board papers, you must **set every partial derivative to zero** and solve the resulting system. Always verify that you have found **all** real solutions — missing a root loses full marks.

## 2.2 Second-Order Conditions (Sufficient Conditions)

Define the **Hessian matrix** $H(\mathbf{x})$:

$$H(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[4pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}_{n \times n}$$

If $f$ is twice continuously differentiable ($C^2$), the Hessian is **symmetric** (Clairaut's theorem).

### The KTU Decision Table (Use This in Every Proof)

| Condition on $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|
| $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite** (PD) | Strict local **MINIMUM** |
| $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **negative definite** (ND) | Strict local **MAXIMUM** |
| $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **indefinite** | **Saddle point** (not an optimum) |
| $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive/negative semi-definite** | **Inconclusive** — need higher-order test |

## 2.3 Definiteness Tests (Sylvester's Criterion)

Let $D_k$ denote the determinant of the leading principal $k \times k$ submatrix of $H$ (the **$k$-th leading principal minor**).

| Definiteness | Criteria |
|---|---|
| Positive Definite (PD) | $D_k > 0$ for all $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| Negative Definite (ND) | $(-1)^k D_k > 0$ for all $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ (i.e., signs alternate: $-, +, -, + \ldots$) |
| Indefinite | $D_n < 0$ and $n$ is odd |
| Semi-definite / Inconclusive | Any $D_k = 0$ |

## 2.4 Quadratic Forms and Definiteness

A **quadratic form** in $n$ variables is:

$$Q(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_i x_j, \quad A = A^T$$

The definiteness of $A$ determines whether the quadratic form is a bowl, dome, or saddle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A matrix $A$ is **PD** iff $Q(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \ne \mathbf{0}$. This is the **definition** — Sylvester's criterion is just a shortcut.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient (Necessary) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Find stationary points |
| Hessian (Sufficient) | $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ definite | Classify optima |
| Sylvester PD | $D_k > 0, \ \forall k$ | Test positive definiteness |
| Sylvester ND | $(-1)^k D_k > 0, \ \forall k$ | Test negative definiteness |
| Convex function | $H(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0$ everywhere | Any local min is global min |
| Concave function | $H(\mathbf{x}) \preceq 0$ everywhere | Any local max is global max |
| Newton's direction | $\mathbf{d} = -[H(\mathbf{x})]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ | Fast local search |
| Steepest Descent | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Slow but robust |
| Armijo step size | $\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha>0} f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ | Line search |
| Wolfe conditions | $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha \nabla f^T \mathbf{d}_k$ and $\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k \vert \le c_2 \vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k \vert$ | Acceptable step size |
| BFGS update | $B_{k+1} = B_k - \dfrac{B_k \mathbf{s}_k \mathbf{s}_k^T B_k}{\mathbf{s}_k^T B_k \mathbf{s}_k} + \dfrac{\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^T}{\mathbf{y}_k^T \mathbf{s}_k}$ | Quasi-Newton inverse Hessian |
| Fletcher–Reeves | $\beta_k = \dfrac{\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert^2}{\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k-1}) \Vert^2}$ | Conjugate gradient |
| Stopping criterion | $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert \le \epsilon$ | Convergence check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Hessian-based second-order test is the gold standard in KTU valuation. Always compute *all* principal minors, not just the determinant.

## 2.6 Convexity — The Shortcut to Global Optima

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** If $f$ is **convex** on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **global minimum**. If $f$ is **concave** and $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **global maximum**.

Equivalent characterisation for $C^2$ functions:
- $f$ is convex $\iff H(\mathbf{x})$ is positive semi-definite for **all** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- $f$ is concave $\iff H(\mathbf{x})$ is negative semi-definite for **all** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

A common sufficient condition: if $H(\mathbf{x})$ is **constant** and PD (or ND), then $f$ is globally convex (or concave).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Classifying a Bivariate Optimum

**Problem:** Find and classify the critical points of
$$f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^3 - 3x_1 x_2^2 + 3 x_1^2 - 3 x_2^2 + 4.$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Gradient

Differentiate partially with respect to $x_1$ and $x_2$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 + 6 x_1$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -6 x_1 x_2 - 6 x_2 = -6 x_2(x_1 + 1)$$

### Step 2 — Set Gradient to Zero (Stationary Points)

From $\partial f / \partial x_2 = 0$:

$$-6 x_2(x_1 + 1) = 0 \implies x_2 = 0 \ \text{or} \ x_1 = -1$$

**Case A:** $x_2 = 0$. Then $\partial f / \partial x_1 = 3x_1^2 + 6 x_1 = 3x_1(x_1 + 2) = 0 \implies x_1 = 0 \ \text{or} \ x_1 = -2$.

This gives two stationary points:
- $\mathbf{x}_A^{(1)} = (0, 0)$
- $\mathbf{x}_A^{(2)} = (-2, 0)$

**Case B:** $x_1 = -1$. Then $\partial f / \partial x_1 = 3(-1)^2 - 3x_2^2 + 6(-1) = 3 - 3x_2^2 - 6 = -3 - 3x_2^2 = -3(1 + x_2^2) = 0$.

But $1 + x_2^2 \ge 1 > 0$ for all real $x_2$, so **no real solution** in Case B.

> **Conclusion:** The only stationary points are $(0,0)$ and $(-2,0)$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Hessian

$$H(x_1, x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 x_1 + 6 & -6 x_2 \\[4pt] -6 x_2 & -6 x_1 - 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Classify $(0, 0)$

$$H(0, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 0 \\ 0 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Leading principal minors:
- $D_1 = 6 > 0$
- $D_2 = (6)(-6) - 0 = -36 < 0$

By Sylvester, $D_2 < 0$ and $n = 2$ is even $\Rightarrow$ **Indefinite** $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point**.

Function value: $f(0,0) = 0 - 0 + 0 - 0 + 4 = 4$.

### Step 5 — Classify $(-2, 0)$

$$H(-2, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} -12 + 6 & 0 \\ 0 & -(-2) - 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Leading principal minors:
- $D_1 = -6 < 0$
- $D_2 = (-6)(-4) - 0 = 24 > 0$

Signs: $D_1 < 0$, $D_2 > 0$ — alternating pattern $(-, +)$ — matches $(-1)^k D_k > 0$ for $k=1,2$.

By Sylvester, **Negative Definite** $\Rightarrow$ Strict Local **MAXIMUM**.

Function value: $f(-2, 0) = (-2)^3 - 0 + 3(4) - 0 + 4 = -8 + 12 + 4 = 8$.

### Step 6 — Summary Table

| Point | $H$ type | $f$ value | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(0, 0)$ | Indefinite | $4$ | **Saddle point** |
| $(-2, 0)$ | Negative Definite | $8$ | **Local Maximum** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Convex Quadratic with PD Hessian

**Problem:** Show that $f(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$, with $A = A^T$ positive definite, has a unique global minimum at $\mathbf{x}^* = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient.

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = A \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$$

(Using the identity $\nabla(\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x}) = (A + A^T)\mathbf{x} = 2A\mathbf{x}$ when $A$ is symmetric, and $\nabla(\mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{b}$.)

**Step 2:** Set the gradient to zero.

$$A \mathbf{x}^* - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0} \implies A \mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}$$

Since $A$ is PD, $\det(A) > 0$, hence $A$ is invertible:

$$\mathbf{x}^* = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Hessian.

$$H(\mathbf{x}) = A \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Step 4:** Since $A$ is PD everywhere, $f$ is strictly convex on $\mathbb{R}^n$, and the unique stationary point is the **global minimum**.

The minimum value is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{b}^T A^{-1} A A^{-1} \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^T A^{-1} \mathbf{b} + c = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{b}^T A^{-1} \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^T A^{-1} \mathbf{b} + c$$

$$f^{**} = c - \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{b}^T A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Newton's Method for Multivariable Optimisation

**Problem:** Find the minimum of $f(x_1, x_2) = 2 x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 - 6 x_1 - 4 x_2 + 13$ using Newton's method, starting at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

### Step 1 — Gradient

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 4 x_1 + 2 x_2 - 6 \\ 2 x_1 + 2 x_2 - 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Hessian (Constant)

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Iterations

Newton direction: $\mathbf{d}_k = -H^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$.

Compute $H^{-1}$:

$$\det(H) = (4)(2) - (2)(2) = 8 - 4 = 4$$

$$H^{-1} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 0:** $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{d}_0 = -H^{-1} \nabla f = -\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} -3 + 2 \\ 3 - 4 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Update with full step ($\alpha = 1$ since $f$ is quadratic):

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1:** $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1)^T$

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 4 + 2 - 6 \\ 2 + 2 - 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Convergence reached in ONE step** (typical for quadratic functions with exact Hessian).

Verify: $f(1,1) = 2(1) + 1 + 2(1)(1) - 6(1) - 4(1) + 13 = 2 + 1 + 2 - 6 - 4 + 13 = 8$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Pure Newton's method converges in **one step** for a quadratic. For non-quadratic functions, it exhibits **quadratic convergence** near the optimum — the number of correct digits doubles each iteration.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Conjugate Gradient (Fletcher–Reeves)

**Problem:** Minimise $f(x_1, x_2) = \dfrac{1}{2}(3 x_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 + 3 x_2^2) - 5 x_1 - 7 x_2$ starting at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

### Setup

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 3 x_1 + x_2 - 5 \\ x_1 + 3 x_2 - 7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad H = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Iteration 0

$$\mathbf{g}_0 = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_0) = \begin{bmatrix} -5 \\ -7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

Optimal line-search step for quadratic ($\alpha = -\dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k}{\mathbf{d}_k^T H \mathbf{d}_k}$):

$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{(-5)(5) + (-7)(7)}{[5, 7] \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}} = -\frac{-25 - 49}{25 \cdot 3 + 7 \cdot 5 + 25 + 49} = \frac{74}{158} = \frac{37}{79}$$

### Iteration 1

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha_0 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 185/79 \\ 259/79 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{g}_1 = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 3(185/79) + 259/79 - 5 \\ 185/79 + 3(259/79) - 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (555 + 259 - 395)/79 \\ (185 + 777 - 553)/79 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 419/79 \\ 409/79 \end{bmatrix}$$

Fletcher–Reeves coefficient:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\Vert \mathbf{g}_1 \Vert^2}{\Vert \mathbf{g}_0 \Vert^2} = \frac{(419)^2 + (409)^2}{(79)^2 \left( 25 + 49 \right)} = \frac{175{,}561 + 167{,}281}{6241 \cdot 74} = \frac{342{,}842}{461{,}834} = \frac{342{,}842}{461{,}834}$$

Continue similarly — for a 2D quadratic, Fletcher–Reeves converges in **at most 2 iterations**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Definiteness of a 3x3 Matrix

**Matrix:**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Leading Principal Minors

$$D_1 = 2 > 0$$

$$D_2 = \det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 4 - 1 = 3 > 0$$

$$D_3 = \det(A)$$

Expanding along row 1:

$$D_3 = 2 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} - (-1) \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + 0$$

$$D_3 = 2(4 - 1) + 1 \cdot (-2 - 0) = 6 - 2 = 4 > 0$$

All three leading principal minors are positive $\Rightarrow$ $A$ is **Positive Definite**.

> **Engineering interpretation:** This matrix arises in 1D finite-difference discretisation of $-\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = f$ — its positive definiteness guarantees unique solution and well-posedness.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow for Classifying Multivariable Optima (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given f x1 x2 ... xn] --> B[Compute partial derivatives]
    B --> C[Solve gradient fx equals 0]
    C --> D{Real solutions exist?}
    D -->|No| E[No stationary point in real domain]
    D -->|Yes| F[Identify candidate points x star]
    F --> G[Compute Hessian matrix H at x star]
    G --> H[Compute leading principal minors D1 D2 ... Dn]
    H --> I{Apply Sylvester criterion}
    I -->|All Dk greater than 0| J[Positive Definite - Local MINIMUM]
    I -->|Signs alternate - + - +| K[Negative Definite - Local MAXIMUM]
    I -->|Dn less than 0 and n odd| L[Indefinite - SADDLE POINT]
    I -->|Some Dk equals 0| M[Inconclusive - Check convexity or higher order]
    J --> N[Check global: H PD everywhere makes f convex]
    K --> O[Check global: H ND everywhere makes f concave]
    L --> P[Saddle - not an optimum]
    M --> Q[Use Taylor expansion or numerical check]
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Selection Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unconstrained Problem] --> B{Function properties}
    B -->|Quadratic with H known| C[Newton Method - 1 step]
    B -->|Smooth nonlinear| D{Second derivatives available}
    D -->|Yes| E[Newton or Quasi-Newton]
    D -->|No| F[Steepest Descent or CG]
    B -->|Noisy or non-differentiable| G[Nelder-Mead Simplex]
    E --> H{Storage constraints}
    H -->|Large n| I[BFGS limited memory]
    H -->|Small n| J[DFP or BFGS full]
    F --> K{Conjugate methods preferred}
    K -->|Yes| L[Fletcher-Reeves CG]
    K -->|No| M[Steepest Descent with line search]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Classification Map (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stationary Point x star] --> B{Definiteness of H at x star}
    B -->|Positive Definite| C[Bowl Shape - Local Minimum]
    B -->|Negative Definite| D[Dome Shape - Local Maximum]
    B -->|Indefinite| E[Saddle Surface - Neither]
    C --> F[All eigenvalues of H greater than 0]
    D --> G[All eigenvalues of H less than 0]
    E --> H[Eigenvalues have mixed signs]
    F --> I[Global if H PD on all of Rn]
    G --> J[Global if H ND on all of Rn]
```

## 4.4 Optimisation Algorithm Iteration Block (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialise x0] --> B[Compute gradient g0]
    B --> C[Set direction d0 equals -g0]
    C --> D[Line search: find alpha k]
    D --> E[Update x k+1 equals xk plus alpha k dk]
    E --> F{Stop test met}
    F -->|No| G[Compute new gradient gk+1]
    G --> H[Compute beta k Fletcher-Reeves or Polak-Ribiere]
    H --> I[Set d k+1 equals -g k+1 plus beta k dk]
    I --> D
    F -->|Yes| J[Return x star]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block diagrams are KTU-valuation-friendly — you can reproduce simplified versions in the exam to score full method marks even if the final numerical answer has a small slip.
