- **Orthogonality** of search directions: in steepest descent, consecutive gradient directions are often nearly parallel → zig-zag. Conjugacy with respect to $Q$ (the *actual* curvature) breaks this correlation.
- **No matrix inversion** of $Q$ is ever required — only matrix–vector products.
- **Restart property**: at iteration $n$ (or any point where $g_{k+1}$ is nearly parallel to $g_0$), reset $d_{k+1} = -g_{k+1}$ to purge accumulated numerical noise.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula (Quadratic Case) | Formula (Non-Quadratic / Line-Search) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Objective | $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^{T} Q x - b^{T} x + c$ | General $f(x)$, $\nabla f$ available |
| 2 | Gradient | $g_k = Q x_k - b$ | $g_k = \nabla f(x_k)$ |
| 3 | Iterative step | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \alpha_k d_k$ | Same |
| 4 | Step length $\alpha_k$ | $\alpha_k = -\dfrac{g_k^{\,T} d_k}{d_k^{\,T} Q d_k}$ | $\alpha_k = \arg\min_\alpha f(x_k + \alpha d_k)$ via line search |
| 5 | Fletcher–Reeves $\beta_k$ | $\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \dfrac{g_{k+1}^{\,T} g_{k+1}}{g_k^{\,T} g_k}$ | Same form |
| 6 | Polak–Ribière $\beta_k$ | — | $\beta_{k+1}^{PR} = \dfrac{g_{k+1}^{\,T}(g_{k+1} - g_k)}{g_k^{\,T} g_k}$ |
| 7 | Direction update | $d_{k+1} = -g_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} d_k$ | Same |
| 8 | Initial direction | $d_0 = -g_0$ | Same |
| 9 | Optimal value | $x^* = Q^{-1} b$ | — |
| 10 | Convergence test | $\Vert g_{k+1} \Vert \leq \varepsilon$ | $\Vert g_{k+1} \Vert \leq \varepsilon$ |
| 11 | Conjugacy check | $d_i^{\,T} Q d_j = 0,\ i \neq j$ | $d_i^{\,T} \nabla^2 f \, d_j \approx 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility** — Conjugate gradient methods are used in:
> - Solving large sparse linear systems $Ax = b$ (CG is the default in PETSc, SciPy).
> - Training neural networks (non-linear CG variants).
> - Structural finite-element analysis, CFD pressure-Poisson equations, and image reconstruction.
> - Quadratic programming and SVM dual problems.

## 2.5 Algorithm Selection Roadmap

| Problem Size / Type | Recommended Method | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Small $n$, exact Hessian | Newton's method | Quadratic convergence |
| Medium $n$, quadratic | Conjugate Gradient | $n$-step exact termination |
| Large / sparse, quadratic | Conjugate Gradient | Only matrix-vector products |
| General nonlinear | Fletcher–Reeves or Polak–Ribière CG | No Hessian needed |
| Ill-conditioned quadratic | **Preconditioned CG (PCG)** | Improves convergence via $M \approx Q$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Step Length $\alpha_k$ (Quadratic)

We want to find $\alpha_k$ minimising $\phi(\alpha) = f(x_k + \alpha d_k)$ along the direction $d_k$ for the quadratic form $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^{T} Q x - b^{T} x + c$.

**Step 1 — Write $\phi(\alpha)$ explicitly.**

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_k + \alpha d_k)^{T} Q (x_k + \alpha d_k) - b^{T}(x_k + \alpha d_k) + c$$

**Step 2 — Expand using $Q = Q^{T}$ and $Q \succ 0$.**

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2} x_k^{T} Q x_k + \alpha d_k^{T} Q x_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 d_k^{T} Q d_k - b^{T} x_k - \alpha b^{T} d_k + c$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha$ and set to zero (first-order necessary condition).**

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = d_k^{T} Q x_k + \alpha\, d_k^{T} Q d_k - b^{T} d_k = 0$$

**Step 4 — Recognise $g_k = Q x_k - b$ and rearrange.**

$$d_k^{T}(Q x_k - b) + \alpha\, d_k^{T} Q d_k = 0$$

$$d_k^{T} g_k + \alpha_k d_k^{T} Q d_k = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the unique minimiser.**

$$\boxed{\alpha_k = -\frac{d_k^{\,T} g_k}{d_k^{\,T} Q \, d_k}}$$

The denominator is strictly positive because $Q \succ 0$ and $d_k \neq 0$, so $\alpha_k$ is well-defined. $\quad \blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fletcher–Reeves $\beta_{k+1}$

**Step 1 — Require that $d_{k+1}$ be Q-conjugate to $d_k$:**

$$d_k^{\,T} Q\, d_{k+1} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the CG recurrence $d_{k+1} = -g_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} d_k$:**

$$d_k^{\,T} Q (-g_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} d_k) = 0$$

$$-d_k^{\,T} Q g_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} d_k^{\,T} Q d_k = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\beta_{k+1}$.**

$$\beta_{k+1} = \frac{d_k^{\,T} Q g_{k+1}}{d_k^{\,T} Q d_k}$$

This is the **Hestenes–Stiefes** form. To remove $Q$, exploit the CG identities $d_k^{T} g_{k+1} = 0$ (orthogonality of successive gradients in CG) and $g_{k+1} = g_k + \alpha_k Q d_k$:

$$d_k^{\,T} Q g_{k+1} = d_k^{\,T} Q (g_k + \alpha_k Q d_k) = d_k^{\,T} Q g_k + \alpha_k (d_k^{\,T} Q d_k)^2 / (d_k^{\,T} Q d_k)$$

Using $d_k^{\,T} Q g_k = d_k^{\,T} Q (Q x_k - b) = d_k^{\,T} Q^2 x_k - d_k^{\,T} Q b$ and the fact that the **residual** $g_{k+1} = Q x_{k+1} - b$ can be related to the previous gradient, the formula collapses cleanly to:

$$\boxed{\beta_{k+1}^{FR} = \frac{g_{k+1}^{\,T} g_{k+1}}{g_k^{\,T} g_k}}$$

This form needs **no** matrix $Q$ at all — only gradient vectors. $\quad \blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style — 14-mark standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem** — Minimise $f(x_1, x_2) = \tfrac{1}{2} x_1^{2} + x_2^{2} - 2 x_1 - 3 x_2 + 4$ using the Conjugate Gradient method, starting from $x_0 = (0, 0)^{T}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Q, b, c matrices.**

$$Q = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad b = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad c = 4, \quad x^* = Q^{-1} b = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 0.**

$$g_0 = Q x_0 - b = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$d_0 = -g_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\alpha_0$.**

Numerator: $g_0^{\,T} d_0 = (-2)(2) + (-3)(3) = -4 - 9 = -13$.

Denominator: $d_0^{\,T} Q d_0 = (2)(1)(2) + (3)(2)(3) = 4 + 18 = 22$.

$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{-13}{22} = \frac{13}{22}$$

**Step 4 — Update $x_1$.**

$$x_1 = x_0 + \alpha_0 d_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \frac{13}{22}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 26/22 \\ 39/22 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 13/11 \\ 39/22 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute new gradient $g_1$.**

$$g_1 = Q x_1 - b = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \cdot \frac{13}{11} - 2 \\ 2 \cdot \frac{39}{22} - 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{13 - 22}{11} \\ \frac{78 - 66}{22} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -9/11 \\ 12/22 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -9/11 \\ 6/11 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\beta_1$ (Fletcher–Reeves).**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{g_1^{\,T} g_1}{g_0^{\,T} g_0} = \frac{(-9/11)^2 + (6/11)^2}{(-2)^2 + (-3)^2} = \frac{(81 + 36)/121}{4 + 9} = \frac{117/121}{13} = \frac{9}{121}$$

**Step 7 — Compute new direction $d_1$.**

$$d_1 = -g_1 + \beta_1 d_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 9/11 \\ -6/11 \end{pmatrix} + \frac{9}{121}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 9/11 + 18/121 \\ -6/11 + 27/121 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{pmatrix} (99 + 18)/121 \\ (-66 + 27)/121 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 117/121 \\ -39/121 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Compute $\alpha_1$.**

Numerator: $g_1^{\,T} d_1 = (-9/11)(117/121) + (6/11)(-39/121) = \frac{-1053 - 234}{1331} = \frac{-1287}{1331}$.

Denominator: $d_1^{\,T} Q d_1 = (117/121)^2 \cdot 1 + (-39/121)^2 \cdot 2 = \frac{13689 + 3042}{14641} = \frac{16731}{14641}$.

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{1287/1331}{16731/14641} = \frac{1287 \times 14641}{1331 \times 16731} = \frac{1287 \times 121}{11 \times 16731} = \frac{1287 \times 11}{16731} = \frac{11}{13}$$

(Here we used $1287 = 9 \cdot 11 \cdot 13$ and $16731 = 11 \cdot 39^2 = 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 169 = 11 \cdot 9 \cdot 13^2$.)

**Step 9 — Update $x_2$.**

$$x_2 = x_1 + \alpha_1 d_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 13/11 \\ 39/22 \end{pmatrix} + \frac{11}{13} \begin{pmatrix} 117/121 \\ -39/121 \end{pmatrix}$$

First component:

$$\frac{13}{11} + \frac{11 \cdot 117}{13 \cdot 121} = \frac{13}{11} + \frac{117}{11 \cdot 13} = \frac{13^2 + 117}{11 \cdot 13} = \frac{169 + 117}{143} = \frac{286}{143} = 2$$

Second component:

$$\frac{39}{22} + \frac{11 \cdot (-39)}{13 \cdot 121} = \frac{39}{22} - \frac{39}{13 \cdot 11} = \frac{39}{22} - \frac{39}{143} = \frac{39 \cdot 13 - 78}{286} = \frac{507 - 78}{286} = \frac{429}{286} = 1.5$$

**Step 10 — Verify convergence.**

$$x_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1.5 \end{pmatrix} = Q^{-1} b = x^* \quad \checkmark$$

$g_2 = Q x_2 - b = 0$, so the method terminated in **$n = 2$ iterations**, exactly as the theory predicts. The minimum value is:

$$f(x^*) = \tfrac{1}{2}(2^2) + (1.5)^2 - 2(2) - 3(1.5) + 4 = 2 + 2.25 - 4 - 4.5 + 4 = -0.25$$

## 3.4 Convergence-Rate Analysis

For a quadratic with condition number $\kappa = \lambda_{\max}(Q) / \lambda_{\min}(Q)$:

$$\frac{\Vert x_{k+1} - x^* \Vert_Q}{\Vert x_0 - x^* \Vert_Q} \leq \left( \frac{\sqrt{\kappa} - 1}{\sqrt{\kappa} + 1} \right)^{k}$$

The effective rate is $\frac{\sqrt{\kappa} - 1}{\sqrt{\kappa} + 1}$, which is **dramatically smaller** than the steepest-descent rate $\frac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}$. This is why CG is the preferred Krylov-subspace method for ill-conditioned systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of the Conjugate Gradient Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Choose initial x0, tolerance epsilon])
    nodeB[/Compute g0 = grad f at x0/]
    nodeC[Set d0 = - g0]
    nodeD[/Compute step length alpha_k via line search or quadratic formula/]
    nodeE[Update x k plus 1 = x k + alpha k d k]
    nodeF[/Compute new gradient g k plus 1 at x k plus 1/]
    nodeG{Convergence test: norm g k plus 1 less than epsilon}
    nodeH[STOP: return x k plus 1 as optimum]
    nodeI{k equals n minus 1}
    nodeJ[Set d k plus 1 = - g k plus 1 and reset counter]
    nodeK[/Compute beta k plus 1 = g k plus 1 transpose g k plus 1 over g k transpose g k/]
    nodeL[Set d k plus 1 = - g k plus 1 + beta k plus 1 d k]
    nodeM[Increment k to k plus 1 and loop back to step length computation]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- NO, continue --> nodeI
    nodeG -- YES, converged --> nodeH
    nodeI -- YES, restart --> nodeJ
    nodeI -- NO, continue --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeD
```

## 4.2 Comparison: Steepest Descent vs. Conjugate Gradient — Trajectory Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SD[STEEPEST DESCENT]
        nodeS1((X0)) -- g0 direction --> nodeS2((X1)) -- perpendicular to g1 --> nodeS3((X2)) -- g2 --> nodeS4((X3)) -- g3 --> nodeS5((X star))
    end

    subgraph CG[CONJUGATE GRADIENT]
        nodeC1((X0)) -- d0 conjugate --> nodeC2((X1)) -- d1 conjugate --> nodeC3((X star))
    end

    classDef startClass fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef starClass fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
    class nodeS1,nodeC1 startClass
    class nodeS5,nodeC3 starClass
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Steepest-descent zig-zags many times to reach $x^\star$ (its successive gradients are perpendicular, but the trajectory is wasteful). Conjugate gradient reaches $x^\star$ in **at most $n$ steps** because its directions are Q-conjugate — each step removes a full "direction of error" from the search.

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of a CG Iteration (Function-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK[INPUT LAYER]
        nodeA1[Current iterate x k]
        nodeA2[Previous gradient g k]
        nodeA3[Previous direction d k]
        nodeA4[Hessian matrix Q]
    end

    subgraph CORE_BLOCK[CG COMPUTATION CORE]
        nodeB1[Step length: alpha k = minus g k transpose d k over d k transpose Q d k]
        nodeB2[Position update: x k plus 1 = x k + alpha k d k]
        nodeB3[Gradient update: g k plus 1 = Q x k plus 1 minus b]
        nodeB4[Beta update: beta k plus 1 = g k plus 1 transpose g k plus 1 over g k transpose g k]
        nodeB5[Direction update: d k plus 1 = minus g k plus 1 + beta k plus 1 d k]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK[OUTPUT LAYER]
        nodeC1[New iterate x k plus 1]
        nodeC2[New direction d k plus 1]
        nodeC3[Convergence flag]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA4 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2
    nodeA3 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeA4 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeA2 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5
    nodeB3 --> nodeB5
    nodeA3 --> nodeB5
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeB5 --> nodeC2
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Architecture Insight**: Notice the **Information Flow** — the matrix $Q$ is touched only via two operations: $d_k^{T} Q d_k$ (a scalar) and $Q x_{k+1}$ (a matrix–vector product). **No inversion, no factorisation.** This is the engineering miracle that makes CG the workhorse of large-scale scientific computing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define conjugate directions. State the conjugate direction theorem.**

**Model Answer:**
A set of non-zero vectors $d_0, d_1, \ldots, d_{n-1}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ are said to be **mutually Q-conjugate** with respect to a positive definite matrix $Q$ if $d_i^{\,T} Q d_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

**Conjugate Direction Theorem:** If a quadratic function $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^{T} Q x - b^{T} x + c$ is minimised sequentially along a set of $n$ mutually Q-conjugate directions, the minimum is reached in **at most $n$ steps**, irrespective of the starting point.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Write the general step of the Conjugate Gradient algorithm for a quadratic function. Explain the role of $\beta_k$.**

**Model Answer:**
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \alpha_k d_k, \quad \alpha_k = -\frac{d_k^{\,T} g_k}{d_k^{\,T} Q d_k}$$
$$d_{k+1} = -g_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} d_k, \quad \beta_{k+1} = \frac{g_{k+1}^{\,T} g_{k+1}}{g_k^{\,T} g_k} \text{ (Fletcher-Reeves)}$$

**Role of $\beta_k$:** The coefficient $\beta_k$ injects a "memory" of the previous direction $d_{k-1}$ into the new direction $d_k$, so that the new search direction remains **Q-conjugate** to all previous ones. It ensures the algorithm does not revisit directions in which the function has already been minimised.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** *Explain the Conjugate Direction method for unconstrained optimisation. Prove that when applied to a quadratic function, the method converges in at most $n$ iterations.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Using the Fletcher–Reeves Conjugate Gradient method, minimise $f(x_1, x_2) = \tfrac{1}{2} x_1^{2} + x_2^{2} - 2 x_1 - 3 x_2 + 4$ starting from $x_0 = (0, 0)^{T}$. Verify convergence.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Algorithm Statement.**
The Conjugate Direction method generates iterates via $x_{k+1} = x_k + \alpha_k d_k$ where $\{d_0, d_1, \ldots\}$ is a set of non-zero, mutually Q-conjugate directions and $\alpha_k$ minimises $f$ along $d_k$.

**Proof of $n$-step termination.**

Consider the quadratic $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^{T} Q x - b^{T} x + c$ with $Q \succ 0$, and the expansion point $x_0$. Define the error $e_k = x_k - x^*$. We claim:

$$e_k \in \text{span}\{d_0, d_1, \ldots, d_{k-1}\}^\perp \text{ (Q-orthogonal complement)}$$

*Proof by induction.*

**Base case ($k = 0$):** Trivially, $x_0 = x^* + e_0$ so $e_0$ spans the trivial subspace; nothing to show.

**Inductive step:** Suppose $e_k \perp_Q \{d_0, \ldots, d_{k-1}\}$. After minimising along $d_k$, we have

$$g(x_{k+1})^{T} d_j = 0 \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, \ldots, k$$

Now, $g(x_{k+1}) = Q e_{k+1}$ (since $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ gives $g(x) = Q(x - x^*)$). Hence

$$d_j^{\,T} Q e_{k+1} = 0 \quad \text{for all } j = 0, \ldots, k$$

Since $\{d_0, \ldots, d_{n-1}\}$ span $\mathbb{R}^n$ (Q-conjugacy implies linear independence), after $n$ such steps:

$$d_j^{\,T} Q e_n = 0 \quad \text{for a basis of } \mathbb{R}^n \Rightarrow e_n = 0 \Rightarrow x_n = x^*$$

Hence, **$n$ iterations suffice**. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

This is the worked numerical example in §3.3 of these notes. Follow those 10 steps and present:

| Iteration | $x_k$ | $g_k$ | $d_k$ | $\alpha_k$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 0 | $(0, 0)$ | $(-2, -3)$ | $(2, 3)$ | $13/22$ |
| 1 | $(13/11, 39/22)$ | $(-9/11, 6/11)$ | $(117/121, -39/121)$ | $11/13$ |
| 2 | $(2, 1.5)$ | $(0, 0)$ | — | — |

Final optimum $x^* = (2, 1.5)^{T}$, $f(x^*) = -0.25$. Converged in $n = 2$ steps, exactly as predicted.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** *Derive the optimal step-length formula $\alpha_k = - \dfrac{d_k^{\,T} g_k}{d_k^{\,T} Q d_k}$ for minimising a quadratic along a search direction $d_k$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Using the Conjugate Gradient method, minimise $f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 + (x_3 - 3)^2 + 5$ starting from $x_0 = (0, 0, 0)^{T}$. Justify why the answer is reached in at most 3 steps.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

For the quadratic $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^{T} Q x - b^{T} x + c$, consider the 1-D function

$$\phi(\alpha) = f(x_k + \alpha d_k)$$

Substituting and expanding:

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_k + \alpha d_k)^{T} Q (x_k + \alpha d_k) - b^{T}(x_k + \alpha d_k) + c$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = d_k^{\,T} Q (x_k + \alpha d_k) - b^{T} d_k = 0$$

$$d_k^{\,T} (Q x_k - b) + \alpha_k\, d_k^{\,T} Q d_k = 0$$

Since $g_k = Q x_k - b$, and the second-order derivative $\phi''(\alpha) = d_k^{\,T} Q d_k > 0$ (positive-definiteness of $Q$):

$$\boxed{\alpha_k = -\frac{d_k^{\,T} g_k}{d_k^{\,T} Q d_k}}$$

This $\alpha_k$ is the **unique global minimiser** of $\phi(\alpha)$ along $d_k$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Rewrite $f$ in quadratic form.**

$$f(x) = x_1^2 - 2x_1 + 1 + x_2^2 - 4x_2 + 4 + x_3^2 - 6x_3 + 9 + 5$$

$$= \tfrac{1}{2} x^{T} (2 I_3) x - (1, 2, 3) \cdot x + 19$$

So $Q = 2 I_3$, $b = (1, 2, 3)^{T}$, $c = 19$, and the true minimum is at $x^* = Q^{-1} b = (0.5, 1, 1.5)^{T}$.

**Step 2 — Notice that $Q$ is a multiple of the identity.** For such "spherical" quadratics, the standard basis vectors are Q-conjugate: $e_i^{\,T} Q e_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 0.**

$$g_0 = Q x_0 - b = (0,0,0) - (1, 2, 3) = (-1, -2, -3)$$

$$d_0 = -g_0 = (1, 2, 3)$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\alpha_0$.**

$$d_0^{\,T} Q d_0 = 2(1 + 4 + 9) = 28, \quad d_0^{\,T} g_0 = -14$$

$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{-14}{28} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Update.**

$$x_1 = (0, 0, 0) + \tfrac{1}{2}(1, 2, 3) = (0.5, 1, 1.5)$$

**Step 6 — Verify.**

$$g_1 = Q x_1 - b = (1, 2, 3) - (1, 2, 3) = (0, 0, 0) = 0$$

The method converged in **1 step** (not even 3!), because for a perfectly conditioned quadratic ($\kappa = 1$), CG finishes immediately at the optimum. The bound "$n$ steps" is an **upper** bound.

**Justification of $n$-step bound:** By the Conjugate Direction Theorem proved in Q-A(a), $n$ Q-conjugate directions span $\mathbb{R}^n$, so any conjugate-direction algorithm (including CG) reaches $x^*$ in at most $n = 3$ iterations. Here, $x_1$ already equals $x^*$, confirming the theorem.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Sign error in $\alpha_k$:** Many students write $\alpha_k = +\frac{d_k^T g_k}{d_k^T Q d_k}$, **forgetting the minus sign** derived from the first-order optimality condition. Deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $g_k^T g_k$ in the denominator of $\alpha_k$:** This is for steepest descent, not CG. CG requires $d_k^T Q d_k$ in the denominator (quadratic case).
> 3. **Conflating Fletcher-Reeves with Polak-Ribière:** These are **two different formulas** for $\beta_k$. State clearly which one is used.
> 4. **Forgetting the restart condition:** When $k = n$, the gradient $g_n$ may be parallel to $g_0$, so the direction must be reset to $d_n = -g_n$.
> 5. **Numerical computation in non-simplified fractions:** KTU evaluators reward clean fraction arithmetic. Simplify $\frac{26}{22}$ to $\frac{13}{11}$ immediately.
> 6. **Not stating the convergence criterion:** Always finish with "$g_{k+1} = 0$, hence $x_{k+1} = x^*$".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Conjugacy Condition:** $d_i^{\,T} Q d_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$ defines Q-orthogonality.
>
> ✅ **Optimal step length (quadratic):** $\alpha_k = -\dfrac{d_k^{\,T} g_k}{d_k^{\,T} Q d_k}$.
>
> ✅ **Fletcher–Reeves $\beta$:** $\beta_{k+1} = \dfrac{g_{k+1}^{\,T} g_{k+1}}{g_k^{\,T} g_k}$.
>
> ✅ **Polak–Ribière $\beta$ (for non-quadratics):** $\beta_{k+1} = \dfrac{g_{k+1}^{\,T}(g_{k+1} - g_k)}{g_k^{\,T} g_k}$.
>
> ✅ **Direction update:** $d_{k+1} = -g_{k+1} + \beta_{k+1} d_k$.
>
> ✅ **Initial direction:** $d_0 = -g_0$ (steepest descent).
>
> ✅ **n-step termination:** Any conjugate-direction method on a quadratic in $\mathbb{R}^n$ converges in **at most $n$ steps**.
>
> ✅ **Restart condition:** Every $n$ iterations (or when $g_{k+1} \approx g_0$), reset $d_{k+1} = -g_{k+1}$.
>
> ✅ **No matrix inversion:** CG needs only $Q x$ products and $Q d$ products — making it ideal for large sparse systems.
>
> ✅ **Better than steepest descent:** CG's effective rate $\dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa} - 1}{\sqrt{\kappa} + 1}$ is **much smaller** than steepest-descent's $\dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}$ for ill-conditioned problems.
>
> ✅ **Preconditioning:** For very large or ill-conditioned systems, use **PCG** with a preconditioner $M \approx Q$ to cluster eigenvalues.
>
> ✅ **Optimal solution of a quadratic:** $x^* = Q^{-1} b$ — always verify against this at the end!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constrained Optimization Methods: Lagrange Multipliers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constrained Optimization Methods: Lagrange Multipliers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Constrained Optimization** is the process of optimizing (maximizing or minimizing) an **objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to one or more **constraints** $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ that the decision variables must satisfy. The **Lagrange Multiplier Method**, developed by the Italian-French mathematician **Joseph-Louis Lagrange** in his 1788 treatise *Mécanique Analytique*, is the foundational analytical strategy for solving **equality-constrained** nonlinear programming problems.

Mathematically, the general constrained problem is stated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\
\text{subject to } \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) = b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& \text{where } m < n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBIET504 - Module 3):** The Lagrange Multiplier method applies to problems with **only equality constraints** of the form $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$. For **inequality constraints** ($\leq$ or $\geq$), the **Kuhn-Tucker conditions** (Module 3 extended topic) are required.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**"The Hiker on a Foggy Mountain"**

Imagine you are a hiker climbing a mountain (objective: maximize altitude) but you are **required to stay on a specific marked trail** (constraint). You cannot just walk in the direction of steepest ascent — you are restricted to the trail.

- The **trail** is your constraint $g(x, y) = c$.
- The **altitude map contours** represent your objective function $f(x, y)$.
- The **optimal point** is where the trail is **tangent** to an altitude contour, meaning the trail neither gains nor loses elevation locally — but at the highest possible point on the trail.

The **Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$** is essentially the **"rate of trade-off"** — it tells you *how much the objective would improve if you could relax the constraint slightly*. In economics, this is famously called the **shadow price**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we cannot just substitute?** In simple cases, we can solve $g(x,y) = c$ for $y$ and substitute into $f$. But for complex nonlinear systems or many variables, this becomes impossible. The Lagrange method provides a *systematic, symmetric* approach that scales to high dimensions.

## 1.3 Standard Canonical Form (KTU Convention)

For a two-variable problem with one equality constraint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } & f(x, y) \\
\text{subject to } & g(x, y) = b
\end{aligned}
$$

The augmented function, called the **Lagrangian function** $\mathcal{L}$, is constructed as:

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = f(x, y) - \lambda\,[g(x, y) - b]
$$

where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **Lagrange multiplier**. The single scalar $\lambda$ absorbs both the constraint and its right-hand side value $b$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Necessary Conditions — The Heart of the Method

The optimal point $(x^*, y^*, \lambda^*)$ must satisfy the **First-Order Necessary Conditions (FONC)**: every partial derivative of the Lagrangian with respect to *each* decision variable and *each* multiplier must vanish.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = 0
$$

This yields a **system of $(n + m)$ simultaneous equations** in $(n + m)$ unknowns for an $n$-variable, $m$-constraint problem.

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation (Vector Form)

The optimality condition can be written compactly as:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \lambda^* \, \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

This means: **at the optimum, the gradient of the objective function and the gradient of the constraint are parallel (collinear)**. Geometrically, the level curve of $f$ is tangent to the constraint surface at the optimum.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic

1. **Formulate** the problem in standard form with $f$ and $g_j$.
2. **Construct** the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = f - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j$.
3. **Differentiate** $\mathcal{L}$ partially with respect to each $x_i$ and each $\lambda_j$.
4. **Set** all partial derivatives to zero → gives a system of equations.
5. **Solve** the system simultaneously for $x_i^*$ and $\lambda_j^*$.
6. **Verify** the solution using **Second-Order Sufficient Conditions** (Bordered Hessian test) or by inspection.
7. **Interpret** $\lambda^*$ as the sensitivity (shadow price): $\frac{\partial f^*}{\partial b} \approx \lambda^*$.

## 2.4 Second-Order Sufficient Conditions (SOSC)

For a **maximum**, the **Bordered Hessian** matrix $\mathbf{H}_B$ of the Lagrangian (with respect to $x, y$ only) must be:

$$
\mathbf{H}_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & g_x & g_y \\ g_x & \mathcal{L}_{xx} & \mathcal{L}_{xy} \\ g_y & \mathcal{L}_{yx} & \mathcal{L}_{yy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

- For **maximum**: top-left $1 \times 1$ minor $> 0$ and $\det(\mathbf{H}_B) > 0$.
- For **minimum**: top-left $1 \times 1$ minor $< 0$ and $\det(\mathbf{H}_B) < 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Most numerical problems in PBIET504 ask only for the **first-order stationary point**. Students only lose marks on SOSC when the question explicitly says *"verify whether it is a maximum or minimum."*

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Standard Problem | Optimize $f(x,y)$ s.t. $g(x,y)=b$ | Equality constraint only |
| 2 | Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = f - \lambda [g - b]$ | One $\lambda$ per constraint |
| 3 | FONC | $\nabla f = \lambda \, \nabla g$ | Vector form (geometric) |
| 4 | Component-wise | $f_x = \lambda g_x,\ \ f_y = \lambda g_y$ | For 2-variable case |
| 5 | Constraint Restored | $g(x^*, y^*) = b$ | Always check feasibility |
| 6 | $n$-var, $m$-constr. | $\mathcal{L} = f - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j g_j$ | Gives $(n+m)$ equations |
| 7 | Shadow Price | $\dfrac{\partial f^*}{\partial b} \approx \lambda^*$ | Economic interpretation |
| 8 | Hessian Test | $\det(\mathbf{H}_B) > 0$ (max) or $< 0$ (min) | Bordered Hessian |
| 9 | Constraint Qualification | $\nabla g \neq \mathbf{0}$ at optimum | Regularity condition |
| 10 | Hessian Entry | $\mathcal{L}_{xx} = f_{xx} - \lambda\, g_{xx}$ | Important for SOSC |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Structural Engineering:** Minimizing weight of a truss subject to a stress/stiffness constraint.
- **Operations Research:** Maximizing utility under a budget constraint (consumer theory in microeconomics).
- **Mechanical Design:** Minimizing material cost subject to a fixed load-bearing capacity.
- **Signal Processing:** Optimal filter design under power or bandwidth constraints.
- **Production Planning:** Maximizing output from a fixed amount of raw material or capital.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Necessary Conditions (Two Variables)

We start with:

$$
\text{Maximize } f(x, y) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g(x, y) = b
$$

**Step 1: Total differential of the constraint.**
Since $g(x,y) = b$ is a constant, its total differential is zero:

$$
dg = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\,dy = 0
$$

**Step 2: Total differential of the objective.**
At an interior optimum, $f$ cannot change along the feasible path defined by the constraint. So:

$$
df = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\,dy = 0
$$

**Step 3: Eliminate the differentials.**
From Step 1, solve for $dy$ (assuming $g_y \neq 0$):

$$
dy = -\frac{g_x}{g_y}\,dx
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into $df = 0$.**

$$
f_x\,dx + f_y \left(-\frac{g_x}{g_y}\,dx\right) = 0
$$

**Step 5: Factor out $dx$ (non-zero for movement along constraint).**

$$
\left(f_x - \frac{f_y\, g_x}{g_y}\right) dx = 0
$$

**Step 6: Rearrange.**

$$
f_x\,g_y - f_y\,g_x = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{f_x}{g_x} = \frac{f_y}{g_y} = \lambda
$$

**Step 7: This is precisely the Lagrange condition $\nabla f = \lambda \nabla g$.**

$$
\boxed{f_x = \lambda g_x \quad \text{and} \quad f_y = \lambda g_y, \quad \text{along with} \quad g(x,y) = b}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard KTU 2-Variable Problem

**Problem:** Maximize $f(x, y) = x^2\,y$ subject to $x + y = 6$.

**Step 1: Construct the Lagrangian.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 y - \lambda(x + y - 6)
$$

**Step 2: Take partial derivatives.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2xy - \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = x^2 - \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = -(x + y - 6) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x + y = 6
$$

**Step 3: Equate $\lambda$ from the first two equations.**

$$
2xy = \lambda = x^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2xy = x^2
$$

**Step 4: Solve.** If $x \neq 0$:

$$
2y = x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y = \frac{x}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the constraint.**

$$
x + \frac{x}{2} = 6 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{3x}{2} = 6 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 4
$$

Then $y = 2$, and $\lambda = x^2 = 16$.

**Step 6: Compute the optimum value.**

$$
f^* = (4)^2 (2) = 32
$$

**Step 7: Verify Maximum via Bordered Hessian.**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{xx} = 2y, \quad \mathcal{L}_{yy} = 0, \quad \mathcal{L}_{xy} = 2x
$$

At $(4, 2)$: $\mathcal{L}_{xx} = 4,\ \mathcal{L}_{yy} = 0,\ \mathcal{L}_{xy} = 8$. Also $g_x = 1, g_y = 1$.

$$
\mathbf{H}_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 8 \\ 1 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(\mathbf{H}_B) = 0 - 1(0 - 8) + 1(8 - 4) = 8 + 4 = 12 > 0
$$

With top-left $1 \times 1$ minor $= 0$ (bordered) and the second minor (deleting row 1, col 1):

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 8 \\ 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0 - 64 = -64 < 0
$$

Since the leading principal minors alternate in sign appropriately for a **maximum** in the bordered Hessian convention, $(4, 2)$ is confirmed as a **local maximum**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Economic Application (Cobb-Douglas Utility)

**Problem:** Maximize utility $U(x, y) = x^{0.4} y^{0.6}$ subject to the budget constraint $5x + 3y = 60$.

**Step 1: Lagrangian.**

$$
\mathcal{L} = x^{0.4} y^{0.6} - \lambda(5x + 3y - 60)
$$

**Step 2: FONC.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 0.4\,x^{-0.6} y^{0.6} - 5\lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 0.6\,x^{0.4} y^{-0.4} - 3\lambda = 0
$$

$$
5x + 3y = 60
$$

**Step 3: Divide the first two equations to eliminate $\lambda$.**

$$
\frac{0.4\,x^{-0.6} y^{0.6}}{0.6\,x^{0.4} y^{-0.4}} = \frac{5}{3}
$$

$$
\frac{0.4}{0.6} \cdot \frac{y}{x} = \frac{5}{3} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{y}{x} = \frac{5}{3}
$$

$$
\frac{y}{x} = \frac{5}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y = 2.5\,x
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into budget.**

$$
5x + 3(2.5x) = 60 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 5x + 7.5x = 60 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 12.5x = 60
$$

$$
x^* = 4.8, \quad y^* = 12, \quad \lambda^* = \frac{0.4 \cdot 12}{(4.8)^{0.6} \cdot 5}
$$

**Step 5: Optimal Utility.**

$$
U^* = (4.8)^{0.4} (12)^{0.6} \approx 8.32
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $\lambda^*$:** In economics, $\lambda$ is the **marginal utility of income**. It tells the consumer how much extra utility they would gain from one additional rupee of budget. This is the famous *shadow price* in Lagrange multiplier theory.

## 3.4 Multi-Constraint Extension ($m > 1$)

For the general case with $m$ equality constraints:

$$
\text{Optimize } f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) = b_j,\ \ j=1,\dots,m
$$

The Lagrangian becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j\,[g_j(\mathbf{x}) - b_j]
$$

The FONC become:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \nabla f = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lambda_j \nabla g_j
$$

**Geometric meaning:** The gradient of the objective is now a **linear combination** of the gradients of all active constraints. The Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_j$ are the coefficients of this linear combination.

**Constraint Qualification (CQ):** The gradients $\nabla g_1, \nabla g_2, \ldots, \nabla g_m$ must be **linearly independent** at the optimum; otherwise, the multiplier solution is not unique.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart — Lagrange Multiplier Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Given] --> B[Formulate f and g]
    B --> C[Construct Lagrangian L = f - lambda * g]
    C --> D[Compute Partial Derivatives]
    D --> E["dL/dx = 0"]
    D --> F["dL/dy = 0"]
    D --> G["dL/d(lambda) = 0"]
    E --> H[Solve the System of Equations]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Unique Solution Found?}
    I -->|No| J[Check Constraint Qualification]
    J --> K[Re-examine or Use KKT]
    I -->|Yes| L[Compute lambda-star and f-star]
    L --> M{Question Asks Max or Min?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Apply Bordered Hessian Test]
    N --> O{Determinant Sign Check}
    O -->|Max Confirmed| P[Report Maximum Point]
    O -->|Min Confirmed| Q[Report Minimum Point]
    M -->|No| R[Report Stationary Point]
    P --> S[End]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    K --> S
```

## 4.2 Geometric Block Architecture — Tangency Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Objective Function f x,y] --> B[Level Curves of f]
    C[Constraint g x,y = b] --> D[Constraint Surface]
    B --> E{Tangency Point x-star, y-star}
    D --> E
    E --> F["Gradient f is parallel to Gradient g"]
    F --> G["nabla f = lambda times nabla g"]
    G --> H[First Order Necessary Conditions]
    H --> I[Lagrange Multiplier lambda-star]
    I --> J[Shadow Price Interpretation]
    I --> K[Optimal Value f-star]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Problem Statement] --> Q[Identify Objective f]
    P --> R[Identify Constraint g]
    Q --> S[Form Lagrangian L]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Differentiate]
    T --> U[Set to Zero]
    U --> V[Solve Linear or Nonlinear System]
    V --> W[Stationary Point x-star, lambda-star]
    W --> X[Second Order Test]
    X --> Y[Classify Max or Min]
    Y --> Z[Economic or Engineering Interpretation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway:** The Lagrange multiplier method essentially converts a *constrained* optimization problem into an *unconstrained* one by adding extra variables (the multipliers) and one extra equation per constraint. The method exploits the **tangency** of level curves of $f$ with the constraint surface — a powerful geometric insight that works in any number of dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the necessary conditions for a stationary point of a function $f(x, y)$ subject to the equality constraint $g(x, y) = b$ using the Lagrange multiplier method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Lagrangian function is constructed as:

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = f(x, y) - \lambda\,[g(x, y) - b]
$$

The **First-Order Necessary Conditions (FONC)** for a stationary point are obtained by setting all partial derivatives of $\mathcal{L}$ to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = f_x - \lambda\,g_x = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = f_y - \lambda\,g_y = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = -(g - b) = 0
$$

These give the system $f_x = \lambda g_x$, $f_y = \lambda g_y$, and $g(x,y) = b$. Solving yields the stationary point $(x^*, y^*)$ and the multiplier $\lambda^*$. (1 mark for the Lagrangian, 1 mark for first two FONC, 1 mark for the third equation restoring the constraint.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the economic interpretation of the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ in a constrained utility maximization problem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a utility maximization problem where the consumer maximizes $U(x, y)$ subject to the budget constraint $p_1 x + p_2 y = M$:

1. The Lagrange multiplier $\lambda^*$ represents the **marginal utility of income** (also called the **shadow price** of the budget). (1 mark)
2. Mathematically, $\lambda^* = \dfrac{\partial U^*}{\partial M}$, indicating the rate of change of the optimal utility with respect to a one-unit increase in the budget $M$. (1 mark)
3. It measures the *imputed value* of relaxing the constraint by one unit. If $\lambda^*$ is large, the constraint is binding tightly and extra budget gives substantial utility gain; if $\lambda^* \approx 0$, the constraint is non-binding. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Use the Lagrange multiplier method to solve the following:**

**(a)** Maximize $f(x, y) = 2x + 3y$ subject to $x^2 + y^2 = 13$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm produces two products with cost function $C(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2 - xy$ (in ₹ lakhs), where $x$ and $y$ are output levels. If the firm has a budget of ₹ 10 lakhs, find the production mix that **minimizes** cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Lagrangian.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = 2x + 3y - \lambda(x^2 + y^2 - 13)
$$

**Step 2: FONC.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2 - 2\lambda x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 3 - 2\lambda y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{3}{2\lambda}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = -(x^2 + y^2 - 13) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 + y^2 = 13
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and solve.**

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda^2} + \frac{9}{4\lambda^2} = 13
$$

$$
\frac{4 + 9}{4\lambda^2} = 13 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{13}{4\lambda^2} = 13 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^2 = \frac{1}{4}
$$

$$
\lambda = \pm \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Case 1:** $\lambda = 1/2$ → $x = 2$, $y = 3$, $f = 4 + 9 = 13$ (Maximum).

**Case 2:** $\lambda = -1/2$ → $x = -2$, $y = -3$, $f = -4 - 9 = -13$ (Minimum).

**Answer:** Maximum value is **$f^* = 13$** at $(x^*, y^*) = (2, 3)$ with $\lambda^* = 1/2$. (7 marks: 2 for Lagrangian, 2 for FONC, 2 for solving, 1 for final answer.)

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Formulate the problem.**

Minimize $C(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2 - xy$ subject to $x + y = 10$.

**Step 2: Lagrangian.**

$$
\mathcal{L} = x^2 + 2y^2 - xy - \lambda(x + y - 10)
$$

**Step 3: FONC.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x - y - \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 4y - x - \lambda = 0
$$

$$
x + y = 10
$$

**Step 4: Subtract first from second.**

$$
(4y - x - \lambda) - (2x - y - \lambda) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5y - 3x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{5y}{3}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute into constraint.**

$$
\frac{5y}{3} + y = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{8y}{3} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^* = \frac{30}{8} = 3.75
$$

$$
x^* = \frac{5 \times 3.75}{3} = 6.25
$$

**Step 6: Compute minimum cost.**

$$
C^* = (6.25)^2 + 2(3.75)^2 - (6.25)(3.75) = 39.0625 + 28.125 - 23.4375 = 43.75
$$

**Step 7: Verify minimum via bordered Hessian.**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{xx} = 2,\ \mathcal{L}_{yy} = 4,\ \mathcal{L}_{xy} = -1
$$

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = 8 - 1 = 7 > 0
$$

Positive definite → **Confirmed Minimum**.

**Answer:** Produce $x^* = 6.25$ units and $y^* = 3.75$ units. Minimum cost is **₹ 43.75 lakhs**. (7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for FONC and solution, 2 for verification, 1 for final answer.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Solve using the Lagrange multiplier method:**

**(a)** Minimize $f(x, y, z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2$ subject to $x + y + z = 12$ and $x - y + 2z = 6$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm uses two inputs $x$ and $y$ to produce output with production function $Q = 50 x^{0.6} y^{0.4}$. Input costs are ₹ 4 per unit of $x$ and ₹ 6 per unit of $y$. The firm wants to produce exactly **200 units** at minimum cost. Determine the optimal input combination and minimum cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Lagrangian with two constraints.**

$$
\mathcal{L} = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - \lambda_1(x + y + z - 12) - \lambda_2(x - y + 2z - 6)
$$

**Step 2: FONC.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x - \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 2y - \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = 2z - \lambda_1 - 2\lambda_2 = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve the system along with constraints.**

From the three equations, add the first two:

$$
2x + 2y - 2\lambda_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x + y = \lambda_1
$$

From the constraint $x + y + z = 12$, we get $z = 12 - \lambda_1$.

Subtracting equation 2 from equation 1:

$$
2x - 2y - 2\lambda_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x - y = \lambda_2
$$

From the second constraint: $x - y + 2z = 6$, so $\lambda_2 + 2z = 6$, giving $\lambda_2 = 6 - 2z$.

Solving systematically (full expansion):

$$
x = \frac{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}{2}, \quad y = \frac{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}{2}, \quad z = \frac{\lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2}{2}
$$

Substitute into the first constraint:

$$
\frac{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}{2} + \frac{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}{2} + \frac{\lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2}{2} = 12
$$

$$
\frac{3\lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2}{2} = 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3\lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = 24
$$

Substitute into the second constraint:

$$
\frac{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}{2} - \frac{\lambda_1 - \lambda_2}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2}{2} = 6
$$

$$
\lambda_2 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 = 6
$$

**Step 4: Solve the $2 \times 2$ linear system.**

$$
3\lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = 24, \quad \lambda_1 + 3\lambda_2 = 6
$$

Multiply the second by 3: $3\lambda_1 + 9\lambda_2 = 18$. Subtract the first:

$$
7\lambda_2 = -6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_2 = -\frac{6}{7}
$$

Then $\lambda_1 = 6 - 3(-6/7) = 6 + 18/7 = 60/7$.

**Step 5: Recover $x, y, z$.**

$$
x = \frac{60/7 - 6/7}{2} = \frac{54/7}{2} = \frac{27}{7} \approx 3.857
$$

$$
y = \frac{60/7 + 6/7}{2} = \frac{66/7}{2} = \frac{33}{7} \approx 4.714
$$

$$
z = \frac{60/7 - 12/7}{2} = \frac{48/7}{2} = \frac{24}{7} \approx 3.429
$$

**Step 6: Minimum value.**

$$
f^* = \left(\frac{27}{7}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{33}{7}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{24}{7}\right)^2 = \frac{729 + 1089 + 576}{49} = \frac{2394}{49} \approx 48.86
$$

**Answer:** $(x^*, y^*, z^*) = (27/7, 33/7, 24/7)$, $f^* \approx 48.86$. (7 marks: 2 for Lagrangian, 3 for solving the linear system, 2 for final values and verification.)

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Formulate the cost-minimization problem.**

Minimize total cost $C = 4x + 6y$ subject to production requirement $50 x^{0.6} y^{0.4} = 200$, i.e., $x^{0.6} y^{0.4} = 4$.

**Step 2: Lagrangian.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = 4x + 6y - \lambda\left(x^{0.6} y^{0.4} - 4\right)
$$

**Step 3: FONC.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 4 - \lambda(0.6)\,x^{-0.4} y^{0.4} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 6 - \lambda(0.4)\,x^{0.6} y^{-0.6} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Divide the two equations.**

$$
\frac{4}{6} = \frac{0.6\,x^{-0.4} y^{0.4}}{0.4\,x^{0.6} y^{-0.6}}
$$

$$
\frac{2}{3} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{y}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{y}{x} = \frac{2}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{4}{9}
$$

$$
y = \frac{4x}{9}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the production constraint.**

$$
x^{0.6} \left(\frac{4x}{9}\right)^{0.4} = 4
$$

$$
x^{0.6} \cdot \frac{4^{0.4}}{9^{0.4}} \cdot x^{0.4} = 4
$$

$$
x \cdot \frac{4^{0.4}}{9^{0.4}} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{4 \cdot 9^{0.4}}{4^{0.4}} = 4 \cdot \left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^{0.4}
$$

Computing: $(9/4)^{0.4} = (2.25)^{0.4} \approx 1.4045$. So $x^* \approx 5.618$.

Then $y^* = \frac{4 \times 5.618}{9} \approx 2.497$.

**Step 6: Minimum cost.**

$$
C^* = 4(5.618) + 6(2.497) = 22.472 + 14.982 = 37.45
$$

**Answer:** Use $x^* \approx 5.62$ units of input 1 and $y^* \approx 2.50$ units of input 2. Minimum cost is **₹ 37.45**. (7 marks: 2 for formulation, 2 for FONC and ratio, 2 for numerical solution, 1 for final cost.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint equation** in the FONC set — many students write $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x = 0$ and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial y = 0$ but **omit** $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \lambda = 0$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention** for the Lagrangian: KTU accepts both $\mathcal{L} = f - \lambda g$ and $\mathcal{L} = f + \lambda g$, but **$\lambda$ will have opposite signs**. Be consistent.
> 3. **Not verifying** whether the stationary point is a max or min when the question asks — lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Algebraic errors** in multi-constraint problems (3 variables, 2 constraints) — set up the linear system in $\lambda$ carefully.
> 5. **Forgetting to substitute back** to find $f^*$ — many students stop at $x^*$ and $y^*$.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lagrangian construction:** $\mathcal{L} = f - \lambda(g - b)$ — *one multiplier per equality constraint*.
- **First-Order Necessary Conditions:** All $(n + m)$ partial derivatives of $\mathcal{L}$ equal zero.
- **Geometric meaning:** $\nabla f = \lambda \nabla g$ → level curves of $f$ are *tangent* to the constraint at the optimum.
- **Always check the constraint qualification:** $\nabla g \neq \mathbf{0}$ at the optimum.
- **Shadow price interpretation:** $\lambda^* = \partial f^*/\partial b$ → measures the marginal value of relaxing the constraint.
- **Bordered Hessian test:** Use only when verification of max/min is asked. For 2 variables, check the sign of the determinant of the $2 \times 2$ Hessian of $\mathcal{L}$.
- **Substitution method** is an alternative but fails for complex nonlinear constraints — Lagrange method is **systematic and scalable**.
- **Multiple constraints:** Use one $\lambda$ per constraint; the gradient of $f$ is a **linear combination** of constraint gradients.
- **For inequality constraints** ($g \leq b$ or $g \geq b$), the method **does not directly apply** — use **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** instead (covered in the next topic).
- **Common exam tip:** If the question says "show that the optimum is at point P", substitute P back into FONC and solve for $\lambda$ — this is the fastest way.
- **Kerala-specific KTU trend (2022–2024):** More weightage on **3-variable, 2-constraint** problems and **economic applications** (utility, cost minimization, production). Expect one 14-mark question per semester on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kuhn-Tucker Condition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kuhn-Tucker Condition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Kuhn-Tucker Conditions (KTC)**, also known as the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions**, constitute the foundational first-order necessary optimality criteria for a **non-linear programming (NLP) problem** that contains **inequality and/or equality constraints**. They generalize the classical method of Lagrange multipliers by incorporating multipliers (called **Kuhn-Tucker multipliers**) that act as "shadow prices" or "sensitivity measures" for the active constraints at the optimal point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Form of a Non-Linear Programming Problem (KTU Convention)**
>
> Minimize $Z = f(\mathbf{x}) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$
>
> Subject to the inequality constraints:
> $$g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$
>
> Subject to the equality constraints:
> $$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$
>
> with $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and the implicit assumption $\mathbf{x} \ge \mathbf{0}$ is encoded as additional $-x_k \le 0$ constraints.

The point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is said to be a **Kuhn-Tucker point** (or **KT-point**) if there exist multiplier vectors $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^* \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* \in \mathbb{R}^p$ such that the **stationarity, complementary slackness, feasibility, and dual feasibility** conditions are all simultaneously satisfied.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Hiking Trip with Budget, Time, and Terrain Limits"**
>
> Imagine you are planning the *cheapest* hiking trip from Base Camp to Peak Camp. You must:
> - **Objective**: Minimize total cost (food, fuel, gear) — this is $f(\mathbf{x})$.
> - **Hard limit 1**: Pack weight must not exceed 12 kg ($g_1(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$).
> - **Hard limit 2**: Trip duration must equal exactly 4 days ($h_1(\mathbf{x}) = 0$).
> - **Hard limit 3**: Elevation gain must be ≤ 2000 m ($g_2(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$).
>
> The **Kuhn-Tucker multiplier** $\lambda_i$ for the pack-weight constraint tells you: *"If the airline relaxed your weight limit by 1 kg, how much money would you save?"* If $\lambda_i = 0$, the constraint is **slack (non-binding)** — it does not pinch the optimum. If $\lambda_i > 0$, the constraint is **active (binding)** — it is genuinely limiting your optimal plan.
>
> This is exactly what the KT-conditions formalize: at the optimum, every constraint is either *binding* (with a positive "pressure" multiplier) or *not binding* (with zero pressure). The gradient of the objective must be perfectly counter-balanced by the gradients of the active constraints.

## 1.3 Why KT-Conditions Matter in KTU Syllabus

- **Engineering utility**: Resource allocation in mechanical design (minimize weight subject to strength constraints), portfolio optimization in finance, optimal control in robotics, and chemical process optimization.
- **Mathematical beauty**: They reduce a constrained optimization problem to a system of algebraic equations solvable by calculus.
- **Generalized framework**: They unify three classical ideas — *unconstrained calculus* ($\nabla f = 0$), *equality-constrained Lagrange* ($\nabla f + \sum \mu \nabla h = 0$), and *inequality handling* (via complementary slackness).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Four Pillars of KTC**: **"S-F-C-D"**
> 1. **S**tationarity of the Lagrangian
> 2. **F**easibility (primal)
> 3. **C**omplementary slackness
> 4. **D**ual feasibility (multiplier signs)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructing the Lagrangian Function

For a minimization problem, the **Lagrangian function** $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu})$ is constructed by augmenting the objective with a *penalty-weighted sum* of constraint violations:

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \, h_j(\mathbf{x})$$

The multipliers $\lambda_i$ and $\mu_j$ are **auxiliary variables** we solve for simultaneously with $\mathbf{x}$.

## 2.2 Statement of the Kuhn-Tucker Theorem (Necessary Conditions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kuhn-Tucker Necessary Theorem (KTU Board Standard Statement)**
>
> Let $f(\mathbf{x})$, $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ ($i = 1, \ldots, m$), and $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ ($j = 1, \ldots, p$) be continuously differentiable functions on an open set containing the feasible region. Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be a **local minimum** of $f$ subject to the constraints, and suppose that $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **regular point** (i.e., the gradients $\nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for all active inequality constraints and $\nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for all equality constraints are linearly independent).
>
> Then there **exist** vectors $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^* = (\lambda_1^*, \ldots, \lambda_m^*)$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}^* = (\mu_1^*, \ldots, \mu_p^*)$ such that the following four sets of conditions hold:

### The Four Pillars of KTC

**Pillar 1 — Stationarity (Gradient of Lagrangian vanishes):**

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \, \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \, \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Pillar 2 — Complementary Slackness (The Signature KTC Condition):**

$$\lambda_i^* \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

**Pillar 3 — Dual Feasibility (Multiplier Sign Condition):**

$$\lambda_i^* \ge 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \quad \text{(sign unrestricted for } \mu_j\text{)}$$

**Pillar 4 — Primal Feasibility:**

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

## 2.3 Intuition Behind Each Pillar

| Pillar | Engineering Intuition | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|
| Stationarity | At optimum, no small nudge improves $f$; constraint gradients exactly balance $\nabla f$. | Sets up $n$ simultaneous equations. |
| Complementary Slackness | A constraint that is *not* binding contributes *zero* shadow price. A binding constraint may have positive price. | Couples $\lambda_i$ and $g_i$ — the heart of KTC. |
| Dual Feasibility | Penalty multipliers for $\le 0$ inequality constraints cannot be negative (else objective improves by violating the constraint). | Restricts search region for $\lambda_i$. |
| Primal Feasibility | The candidate solution must itself satisfy all constraints. | Filters out non-feasible points. |

## 2.4 Sufficient Kuhn-Tucker Theorem (Convex Programming)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sufficient Theorem (KTU Board Exam Favourite)**
>
> If, in addition to the necessary conditions:
> - $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a **convex function**,
> - each $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ is a **convex function** (i.e., feasible region $\{ \mathbf{x} : g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0 \}$ is convex),
> - each $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ is a **linear (affine) function**,
>
> then any point $\mathbf{x}^*$ satisfying the KTC is a **global minimum** of the problem.

This is the **convex programming theorem** and is the key reason KTC are routinely applied in engineering design optimization — most practical design problems are convex.

## 2.5 Reformulation Trick: Non-Negativity Constraints

The constraint $x_k \ge 0$ is written in standard form as $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ by:

$$-x_k \le 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{multiplier } \mu_k \ge 0 \text{ in Lagrangian notation}$$

Students commonly confuse signs here. **Memorize**: for a minimization with $g \le 0$, the multiplier $\lambda$ is added (not subtracted) in the Lagrangian.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Symbol / Form | Required Sign / Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard form objective | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ | $f$ convex preferred |
| 2 | Inequality constraint | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | Must be active or slack |
| 3 | Equality constraint | $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Always active |
| 4 | Lagrangian | $L = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ | $\lambda_i \ge 0$, $\mu_j \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Stationarity | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = \mathbf{0}$ | $n$ scalar equations |
| 6 | Complementary slackness | $\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Product must be zero |
| 7 | Dual feasibility (inequality) | $\lambda_i \ge 0$ | Sign-restricted |
| 8 | Dual feasibility (equality) | $\mu_j \in \mathbb{R}$ | Unrestricted |
| 9 | Primal feasibility | $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Always enforced |
| 10 | Convexity sufficient condition | $f$ convex, $g_i$ convex, $h_j$ linear | Guarantees global min |
| 11 | Regular point condition | Active $\nabla g_i, \nabla h_j$ linearly independent | Ensures KTC applicability |
| 12 | Lagrangian magnitude | $\lambda_i = -\partial f / \partial g_i$ (at optimum) | Sensitivity interpretation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: In **structural optimization**, $f(\mathbf{x})$ = mass of the truss; $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ = stress constraints ($\sigma_{allow} - \sigma_i \le 0$); $h_j$ = equilibrium equations. The multiplier $\lambda_i$ literally represents the **shadow price** — the cost of relaxing the stress limit by one unit.

## 2.7 Geometric Interpretation (Board-Exam Worthy)

At the KT-point $\mathbf{x}^*$, the gradient of the objective $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ lies inside the **convex cone** generated by the outward normals of the active constraints. Equivalently, $-\nabla f$ can be expressed as a non-negative combination of the active constraint gradients:

$$-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \sum_{i \in I_{active}} \lambda_i^* \, \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j} \mu_j^* \, \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*)$$

where $I_{active} = \{ i : g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Two-Variable Problem with Two Inequality Constraints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Typical Board Pattern):**
> $$\min \; Z = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2x_1 - 4x_2$$
> Subject to:
> $$x_1 + x_2 \le 2 \quad \text{(Constraint A)}$$
> $$2x_1 + x_2 \le 3 \quad \text{(Constraint B)}$$
> $$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

### Step 1 — Rewrite Constraints in Standard Form $g_i \le 0$

$$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 2 \le 0$$
$$g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 2x_1 + x_2 - 3 \le 0$$
$$g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 \le 0 \quad (\text{encoding } x_1 \ge 0)$$
$$g_4(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 \le 0 \quad (\text{encoding } x_2 \ge 0)$$

> Convention: Multipliers $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3, \lambda_4 \ge 0$ for minimization.

### Step 2 — Construct the Lagrangian

$$L(x_1, x_2, \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3, \lambda_4) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2x_1 - 4x_2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 2) + \lambda_2(2x_1 + x_2 - 3) + \lambda_3(-x_1) + \lambda_4(-x_2)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Stationarity Condition ($\partial L / \partial x_1 = 0$, $\partial L / \partial x_2 = 0$)

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 2 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 - \lambda_3 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 - 4 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 - \lambda_4 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Apply Complementary Slackness

$$\lambda_1 (x_1 + x_2 - 2) = 0$$
$$\lambda_2 (2x_1 + x_2 - 3) = 0$$
$$\lambda_3 (-x_1) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_3 x_1 = 0$$
$$\lambda_4 (-x_2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_4 x_2 = 0$$

### Step 5 — Case Analysis (Searching for Active Constraints)

We know the unconstrained minimizer is at $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 2$ (gradient $= 0$ with all $\lambda_i = 0$). Check feasibility: $x_1 + x_2 = 3 > 2$. **Constraint A is violated.** So Constraint A **must be active** at the optimum. We try $\lambda_1 > 0$.

**Sub-Case 5.1: Only Constraint A active ($\lambda_1 > 0$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda_4 = 0$)**

Setting $x_1 + x_2 = 2$ and using the stationarity equations:

$$2x_1 - 2 + \lambda_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 2 - 2x_1$$

$$2x_2 - 4 + \lambda_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 4 - 2x_2$$

Equating both expressions for $\lambda_1$:

$$2 - 2x_1 = 4 - 2x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 - x_1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = x_1 + 1$$

Substitute into $x_1 + x_2 = 2$:

$$x_1 + (x_1 + 1) = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2x_1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{3}{2}, \qquad \lambda_1 = 2 - 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1$$

**Validate all KTC conditions**:

- **Stationarity**: ✓ (used to derive)
- **Complementary slackness**: $\lambda_1 (x_1 + x_2 - 2) = 1 \cdot 0 = 0$ ✓
- **Dual feasibility**: $\lambda_1 = 1 \ge 0$ ✓
- **Primal feasibility**:
  - $x_1 + x_2 = 2 \le 2$ ✓
  - $2x_1 + x_2 = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5 \le 3$ ✓
  - $x_1 = 0.5 \ge 0$ ✓
  - $x_2 = 1.5 \ge 0$ ✓

All four pillars satisfied! 

**Sub-Case 5.2: Constraints A and B both active ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$)**

The two equality constraints become:

$$x_1 + x_2 = 2, \qquad 2x_1 + x_2 = 3$$

Subtracting: $x_1 = 1$, then $x_2 = 1$.

From stationarity: $\lambda_1 = 2 - 2(1) + 2\lambda_2 \cdot \text{(check sign)} $. Recompute:

$$2x_1 - 2 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = 2 - 2 + \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = \lambda_1 + 2\lambda_2 = 0$$

Since $\lambda_1 \ge 0$ and $\lambda_2 \ge 0$, the only solution is $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0$ — **contradiction**. So this sub-case is infeasible.

### Step 6 — Compute the Optimal Objective Value

$$Z^* = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) - 4\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)$$

$$Z^* = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{9}{4} - 1 - 6 = \frac{10}{4} - 7 = \frac{5}{2} - 7 = -\frac{9}{2}$$

### Final Answer (Example 1)

$$\boxed{\,x_1^* = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad x_2^* = \tfrac{3}{2}, \quad Z_{\min} = -\tfrac{9}{2}, \quad \lambda_1^* = 1, \quad \lambda_2^* = \lambda_3^* = \lambda_4^* = 0\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mixed Equality & Inequality (KTU Advanced Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:**
> $$\min \; Z = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2$$
> Subject to:
> $$2x_1 + x_2 \le 6 \quad (\text{inequality})$$
> $$x_1 + x_2 = 5 \quad (\text{equality})$$
> $$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

### Step 1 — Standardize

$$g_1: 2x_1 + x_2 - 6 \le 0, \qquad h_1: x_1 + x_2 - 5 = 0, \qquad g_2: -x_1 \le 0, \qquad g_3: -x_2 \le 0$$

### Step 2 — Lagrangian

$$L = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2 + \lambda_1(2x_1 + x_2 - 6) + \mu_1(x_1 + x_2 - 5) + \lambda_2(-x_1) + \lambda_3(-x_2)$$

### Step 3 — Stationarity

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 3) + 2\lambda_1 + \mu_1 - \lambda_2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 4) + \lambda_1 + \mu_1 - \lambda_3 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Try Active Set: Equality $h_1$ binding, inequality $g_1$ non-binding

Set $\lambda_1 = 0$, $x_1 + x_2 = 5$, $x_1, x_2 > 0$ (so $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$).

$$2(x_1 - 3) + \mu_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_1 = -2(x_1 - 3) = 6 - 2x_1$$

$$2(x_2 - 4) + \mu_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_1 = 8 - 2x_2$$

Equating:

$$6 - 2x_1 = 8 - 2x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 - x_1 = 1$$

With $x_1 + x_2 = 5$: solve simultaneously:

$$x_1 = 2, \quad x_2 = 3$$

Then $\mu_1 = 6 - 4 = 2$ (free sign — OK).

**Check feasibility**: $2x_1 + x_2 = 4 + 3 = 7 > 6$. So $g_1$ is **violated**. Our assumption $\lambda_1 = 0$ is invalid. We must activate $g_1$.

### Step 5 — Re-Activate $g_1$ ($\lambda_1 > 0$)

Now $2x_1 + x_2 = 6$ **and** $x_1 + x_2 = 5$ both hold. Subtract:

$$x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 4$$

From stationarity:

$$2(1 - 3) + 2\lambda_1 + \mu_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -4 + 2\lambda_1 + \mu_1 = 0$$

$$2(4 - 4) + \lambda_1 + \mu_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 + \mu_1 = 0$$

From the second: $\mu_1 = -\lambda_1$.

Substitute into the first:

$$-4 + 2\lambda_1 - \lambda_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 4$$

Then $\mu_1 = -4$. Sign check: $\lambda_1 = 4 \ge 0$ ✓. $\mu_1$ is unrestricted ✓.

**All KTC satisfied.**

### Step 6 — Compute Minimum Value

$$Z^* = (1 - 3)^2 + (4 - 4)^2 = 4 + 0 = 4$$

### Final Answer (Example 2)

$$\boxed{\,x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 4, \quad Z_{\min} = 4, \quad \lambda_1^* = 4, \quad \mu_1^* = -4\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why is $\mu_1$ negative?** Because we are *minimizing* the squared distance to $(3, 4)$. The equality $x_1 + x_2 = 5$ forces the solution *away* from the unconstrained minimum at $(3, 4)$. A negative multiplier compensates for this displacement, which is perfectly legal since equality-constraint multipliers have **no sign restriction**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Recognition for KTU Problems

| Active Set Configuration | Stationarity Count | Slackness Count | Total Unknowns | Total Equations | Resolution Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Interior point (no binding) | $n$ | $0$ | $n$ | $n$ | Solve $\nabla f = 0$, then check feasibility |
| $k$ inequality active, no equality | $n$ | $k$ | $n + k$ | $n + k$ | Solve $n$ gradient + $k$ active constraints |
| $k$ inequality + $p$ equality active | $n$ | $k$ | $n + k + p$ | $n + k + p$ | System determined; check $\lambda \ge 0$ |
| All constraints active | $n$ | $m + p$ | $n + m + p$ | $n + m + p$ | Over-determined; rarely gives solution |

---

## 3.4 Derivation Outline of KTC (Lagrangian Approach)

For a smooth convex inequality-constrained minimum, we perturb $\mathbf{x}^* \to \mathbf{x}^* + d\mathbf{x}$. Feasibility requires:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}^* + d\mathbf{x}) \approx g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)^T d\mathbf{x} \le 0$$

If $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ (inactive), the perturbation may move in either direction along $\nabla g_i$.
If $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (active), then $\nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*)^T d\mathbf{x} \le 0$.

For optimality, the directional derivative of $f$ along any *feasible* $d\mathbf{x}$ must be non-negative:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T d\mathbf{x} \ge 0 \quad \forall \, d\mathbf{x} \text{ feasible}$$

By the **Farkas lemma** of alternative systems, this implies $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ belongs to the cone generated by $\{\nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) : i \in I_{active}\}$. Hence:

$$-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \sum_{i \in I_{active}} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*), \quad \lambda_i^* \ge 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

with the convention $\lambda_i^* = 0$ for inactive $i$, so the sum runs over all $i$ via complementary slackness. Adding equality constraints with unrestricted multipliers $\mu_j$ gives the full stationarity equation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart — Solving a KTC Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Read NLP problem min f of x"] --> B["Standardize all constraints to g_i of x <= 0 and h_j of x = 0"]
    B --> C["Form Lagrangian L = f + sum lambda_i g_i + sum mu_j h_j"]
    C --> D["Compute partial derivatives: dL/dx_k for k = 1 to n"]
    D --> E["Set partial derivatives to zero: gives n equations"]
    E --> F["Identify candidate active set: which g_i binding at optimum"]
    F --> G["For each candidate active set, solve the system"]
    G --> H{"All four KTC pillars satisfied?"}
    H -- "No, lambda_i < 0" --> I["Discard this active set"]
    H -- "Yes" --> J["Record x star and multipliers"]
    I --> K["Try next active set"]
    K --> H
    J --> L["Compare all feasible KTC candidates"]
    L --> M["Select x star with minimum f value as global min if convex"]
    M --> N["END: Report x star, f min, lambda star, mu star"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic of KTC Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        X1["Objective f of x"]
        X2["Constraints g_i of x <= 0"]
        X3["Constraints h_j of x = 0"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["CORE PROCESSING"]
        Y1["Lagrangian Construction Unit"]
        Y2["Stationarity Solver: grad L = 0"]
        Y3["Slackness and Sign Checker"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        Z1["Optimal x star"]
        Z2["Multiplier vector lambda star"]
        Z3["Multiplier vector mu star"]
        Z4["Optimal objective f star"]
    end
    X1 --> Y1
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Y3
    Y3 -- "All conditions hold" --> Z1
    Y3 -- "All conditions hold" --> Z2
    Y3 -- "All conditions hold" --> Z3
    Y3 -- "All conditions hold" --> Z4
    Y3 -- "Violation detected" --> W["Return INFEASIBLE / NOT KKT"]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Functional Mapping of KTC Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Problem Definition"] --> P2["Constraint Standardization"]
    P2 --> P3["Lagrangian Assembly"]
    P3 --> P4["Stationarity System"]
    P4 --> P5{"Convex Problem?"}
    P5 -- "Yes" --> P6["Sufficient Conditions: Any KTC Point is Global Min"]
    P5 -- "No" --> P7["Necessary Conditions Only: Check All Candidates"]
    P6 --> P8["Output: Global Optimal Solution"]
    P7 --> P9["Enumerate Candidate Active Sets"]
    P9 --> P10["Apply Complementary Slackness"]
    P10 --> P11["Validate Dual Feasibility lambda >= 0"]
    P11 -- "Valid" --> P8
    P11 -- "Invalid" --> P12["Reject Candidate"]
```

## 4.4 Mapping of KTC Pillars to Feasibility Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Stationarity: grad L = 0"] --> R5["Combined KTC System"]
    R2["Primal Feasibility: g <= 0 and h = 0"] --> R5
    R3["Dual Feasibility: lambda >= 0"] --> R5
    R4["Complementary Slackness: lambda g = 0"] --> R5
    R5 --> R6["Intersecting Region Yields KKT Point"]
    R6 --> R7["If convex: x star is Global Minimum"]
```
